KeysToEntries.AshLogs: What ash logs lack in durability, they make up for in availability. Found all over Brynmoor, ash trees became a vital resource to the humans that resided there. Their druids would enchant the products of the trees, so that they did not rot, nor crumble against time’s assault. This would allow the trees to regrow before it would be necessary to cut them down again, creating a sustainable system.
KeysToEntries.OakLogs: Stronger than ash, the oak tree strands proud against its lesser cousin. Oak logs are more durable, sturdier and more desirable for more advanced construction. Though the trees are harder to find in Brynmoor, it is said that Ghornfell offers more of them, if one is willing to face the dangers of exploring it.
KeysToEntries.Stone: Sticks and stones may break your bones, which makes them greatly desired in warfare. Stone can be found everywhere. In the grass, behind bushes, hiding in an uncomfortable part of your shoe just waiting for the right time to strike.
KeysToEntries.Feathers:
The simple feather is the greatest invention of the avian species. The aviansie race is particularly proud of their plumage and hold lengthy conversations about the best maintenance methods. For the average bird, such as magpies and similar, feathers grant them the ability to soar through the skies, defying the whims of gravity.

They are a vital part of crafting arrows, as the feathers are used for the flights that cause the arrow to fly true.
KeysToEntries.AnimalHideScraps: A collection of pelt scraps collected from smaller creatures. Some druids would insist on crafting cloaks made only from smaller animals that died of natural causes. This meant that each cloak often took months, or sometimes even years, to craft. Naturally this wasn’t particularly helpful on the cusp of winter.
KeysToEntries.AnimalHides: Animal hides have a variety of uses and have made hunters very popular throughout history. In Bramblemead there was an annual competition to see who could find the most interesting hide. While anima twisted animals were plentiful, many hunters turned to cheating, leading to a rise in the dye trade.
KeysToEntries.CourseAnimalFur: Bundles of animal fur, scratchy and thick. While not an ideal trait on pets, this sort of coarse fur has a multitude of uses in the wild.
KeysToEntries.AnimalHorn: Nature’s attempt to better the blacksmith’s craft, these powerful horns can do a lot of damage to their target when correctly applied. Fortunately they have a number of uses outside of skewering one’s organs, making them a useful resource prized by all skilled hunters and survivalists.
KeysToEntries.AnimalFangs: Much more fun now that they’ve been removed from the mouth of a particularly angry critter. These teeth are surprisingly sharp, capable of piercing some armour. Scholars argue that the anima twist of Ashenfall makes the teeth more durable, whereas hunters would claim that the animals here are just more naturally belligerent than others. They prefer to use more colourful language to describe them, however.
KeysToEntries.GameMeat: Tender meat, straight from the hunt. Traditionally you would hang the meat to dry out a little before consumption, marinating it in wines, spices and preservatives. However, you can lob it in a fire if you want a more immediate meal. We don’t judge.
KeysToEntries.StringyMeat: Smaller creatures have much less meat on their bones, making them a less fulfilling meal. Hunters would subsist on these while stalking larger prey over several days. The Wild Knight Ilom was particularly fond of drying out these little morsels, treating them as a meaty little snack for boring patrols.
KeysToEntries.FireRunes:
Guthix created runes in the first age, here on Ashenfall. He found a way of capturing the ambient wild anima, of the continent, in the floating rune essence around the region. He would then give these runes to his druids, teaching them the fundamentals of magic.

Fire runes contain enough magical fire to light a campfire. Battlemages prize them for the ability to lob that small fire at an enemy instead. Either way, they’re a very handy resource.
KeysToEntries.AirRunes:
Rune essence has the unique property of absorbing and transforming ambient magic into something that can be used by even races without a natural magical aptitude, like humans.

An air rune contains anima that is aligned with the wind itself. A practitioner of magic can take the rune and convert it into a gentle breeze. Or, with enough of them, a gale force wind.

Very popular in the hot summer months.
KeysToEntries.Flax: Flax was a popular plant during the time of Guthix. Found all over the continent, it could be easily harvested and refined into various materials. Villagers would enjoy the seeds as tasty snacks, but they mostly prized the flax for the threads that could be woven from the stems of the plant.
KeysToEntries.Cabbage:
Few vegetables are as worthy of such reverence as the humble cabbage. Delicious. Easily portable and possessing of a leaf more noble than any other. This, at least, the followers of the god Brassica Prime profess.

No one is entirely sure how there is a god of cabbages. Fewer still are sure why there is a god of cabbages, yet the existence of such a being cannot be denied.

Unlike other deities, who guard the focus of their faith with great jealousy, Brassica Prime delights in nothing more than seeing people enjoying the delicious, nutritious, wonders of the humble cabbage.

Best enjoyed fried or mixed into a stew. Cabbage wine is a failed experiment that should never be spoken of again.
KeysToEntries.Potato:
Baked, fried, mashed, there are few vegetables as versatile as the potato. Buried beneath the soil, these tubers absorb the nutrients around them, ready to be ripped from the soil and consumed.

Some mages noticed that you could pass certain magical currents through them, and one even attempted to enchant one and fashion it into blades. Suffice to say their plan was not successful, but it did create the very first chip, so was largely considered to be a good thing.
KeysToEntries.BirdMeat: The white meat of a bird is surprisingly versatile. While delicious on its own, it can be combined with various dishes, seasoned or marinated to create surprisingly varied flavours. The meat is often softer to consume, making it an easier meal for the on-the-go hunter.
KeysToEntries.AnimalBones: Bones are not just the favoured materials of necromancers, they can be incredibly useful to a skilled survivalist as well. Designed to withstand great force, while still being as light as possible, bones make excellent components in an array of crafting recipes.
KeysToEntries.RuneEssence:
A magical stone with the ability to absorb the ambient anima around it. On Ashenfall, the rocks hover in the air as if resting on some invisible structure, held aloft by invisible currents of magical energy.

With the right equipment and skill, rune essence can be infused with certain magical resonances, taking on the elemental power of the air, or the flame.

This discovery is one of Guthix’s greatest triumphs and perhaps his longest lasting miracle.
KeysToEntries.FarmMeat: This dense meat is a sturdy meal when prepared properly. It comes from larger animals who are far less willing to give it up than their smaller kin, making it a well deserved delicacy.
KeysToEntries.RedBerries: These simple berries might be the gap between life and death, if eaten at the right time. A favourite treat for the villagers of Bramblemead, who would mix the berries into various desserts to enjoy. The red berry pie was particularly popular with visiting Wild Knights.
KeysToEntries.WiseOldMan:
Dionysius was once a legendary adventurer. In his youth, he single-handedly defeated the troll invasion of Keldagrim. He crushed the enemies of one of the mountain tribes and taught the people of Karamja how to use powerful herbs that might aid them in battle. He committed great deeds throughout his life, and the world is a better place with him in it.

Or it was.

After he settled down, finishing his book on destructive magic, Dionysius began to feel that the world was a little unfair. He had tirelessly given his youth for the betterment of the world, and he had nothing to show for it. Even his devout faith in Saradomin wasn’t making his life more comfortable.

So, like so many do, he turned to crime. He targeted the nearby bank, his magic making quick work of their security, and he lifted himself a great deal of gold as well as a snazzy blue party hat. Content to consider this repayment for saving the world so often, he now sits back and enjoys his well-earned wealth.

The Temple Knights consider him an ultraviolet archangel threat and watch him very closely... from very far away.
KeysToEntries.VannakaDefault:
The mysterious warrior, Vannaka, is regarded as one of the premier slayer masters in the world. He trained under the legendary Duradel, and the two even duelled one another once. Duradel won. Something Vannaka has never entirely managed to get over. 

Vannaka’s origins are mysterious. He claims no nation as his homeland, nor does he speak of family or friends. Conversations lead only to talk of combat and the slaying of dangerous creatures. Yet there is something about Ashenfall that creates a silence in him. He stares out across the continent as if searching for something.
KeysToEntries.VannakaWerewolf: Vannaka has shared his greatest secret; he’s a werewolf. It is why Duradel and Kuradel could so quickly best him, for they knew his curse and fought him with wolfbane, stripping him of his strength. He comes to Ashenfall in search of the Garou, the ancestors of the werewolves, so that he might understand his inner beast.
KeysToEntries.Doric:
Doric is a master smith who trained in the dwarven capital of Keldagrim. He owns a smithing shop in Asgarnia alongside his son Boric. The two of them have a strained relationship because Boric took it personally when Doric sent him to Keldagrim to complete his training. 

To rebuild trust and show his son that he has the uttermost faith in his abilities, Doric has left the running of the shop to Boric in his absence. Now, Doric comes to Ashenfall in search of new metals, lost smithing techniques, and the blueprints of ancient weapons. 

Not much of a fighter himself these days, he prefers to stay out of harm’s way, but he knows how to wield a hammer if push comes to shove.
KeysToEntries.Zanik:
Few have lived a life as rich with adventure as the cave goblin Zanik. She was the first of her people to brave the sunlight, the first cave goblin to meet humans and the surface goblins, the first to learn about the gods and their influence on the world, and the first cave goblin to stand side by side with the World Guardian in defence of creation.

It cost her her life. Twice. 

She was once the Chosen Commander of the war god Bandos, who tried to use prophecy to put her under his thrall. She died; he resurrected her and then used magic to seize control. She resisted, fighting off his influence. Bandos’s lingering affliction on her soul would claim her life after the War God’s demise, but even death wouldn’t stop her.  

She became the emissary of the Underworld, resurrected once more by the god Icthlarin, and brought back to the surface to join the fight against the Elder Gods. 

Now, she comes to Ashenfall in search of a lost and forgotten tribe. Hoping to stop history from repeating itself.
KeysToEntries.StaffOfLight:
In the third age, before someone could wield a staff of light, it would first need to be blessed by three people. 

A priest would bless it first, instilling the weapon with Saradomin’s wisdom. A monk would bless it second, imbuing the staff with the humility of Saradomin. Finally, an icyene general would give their blessing, dedicating the weapon to Saradomin’s righteousness. 

Those battlemages chosen to wield such a sacred weapon would dive into battle against Zamorak’s forces and believe themselves invulnerable. 

They were often proved wrong. 

Yet, those who carried the staff into battle seemed to survive longer than their compatriots. The defensive magic of the staff kept them alive longer than others. A demon once stole a staff and tried to use that power for himself. Still, when he tried to channel demonic power through the staff, it is said that the explosion could be seen from across the battlefield.
KeysToEntries.CrystalBow:
Crystal bows are not crafted but sung into existence. Harmonies are layered over one another to teach the weapon a story so that it might understand why it needs to be strong. Traditionally, the plight of the elves, their loneliness without their goddess, and the many dangers they must face are sung. 

The shimmering substance they’re made from is traditionally a shard of the elven goddess Seren, left behind when her crystal form shattered at the end of the Third Age God Wars. 

This bow isn’t the same material, and the song is different, too. There’s no divine essence in the crystal, no lament of the elves. Instead, it is sung memories of a lifetime together, distilled down into facets reflecting in the crystalline flaws. In the moonlight, those facets sparkle like tiny stars held within glass—moments of hardship, conflict, happiness, and grief. 

It is music as a weapon, with memory as the handle and sorrow as the string.
KeysToEntries.BlightwoodLongbow:
The wild creatures of Ashenfall have always been elusive. Bramblemead villagers would tell wild stories about the deer that got away or the sheep that shrugged off an arrow. Still, those who could wield a bow rarely left their family hungry. 

One Wild Knight, Frode, was so skilled with the bow that he never returned from a hunt empty-handed. His siblings among the Wild Knights would send him out hunting before every great gathering so that they might feast heartily upon his return. For years, this was their happiest tradition. 

Until the day he did not return. 

It is said that his body was found in five separate places, spread out across the forests of Brynmoor like some gruesome compass. His quiver was empty, yet none of his arrows – marked as they were, with his symbol, an owl – would ever be found.
KeysToEntries.OakLongbow:
The longbow is every hunter’s friend. Capable of taking down prey over a greater range than the shortbow, it has been the end of many a deer trying to flee through the woods. 

Bramblemead would host a thrilling archery tournament. The villagers would proudly bring their own longbows, meticulously crafted by hand, and compete to take down cleverly hidden targets scattered across the village. It was a spectacle of skill and precision, with the only rule being that the target must be shootable from the open street. 

With the introduction of magic, the competition took on a whole new level of silliness. Participants conjured magical air currents that sent arrows astray – often into unsuspecting washing lines or pies cooling on windowsills – or they would playfully blast arrows out of the air with fire spells. 

However, this stopped after the Amalgamated archer, Derien, joined the game. Gifted with the element of air, his arrows curved gracefully with each shot, missing traps, tricks and deceptions. His victory earned the Amalgamated more jealousy, which led to the competition ending out of envious spite.
KeysToEntries.AshShortbow:
An ash shortbow is the first weapon most young archers train with. It’s easy to make and, therefore, not so problematic if it breaks during training. Human villagers would often craft their first bows together under the tutelage of a visiting Wild Knight. They would use branches that had naturally fallen from the trees and carefully whittle them into the bow’s limbs. Bowstrings would be created by gathering flax from the nearby fields and spinning them into thin but hardy threads. 

Most would break on first use. Some would break before the first arrow was fired. Still, village youngsters would laugh at their misfortunes and start the whole process again. Each time improving. Each new bow sturdier than the last.
KeysToEntries.OakShortbow:
The villagers who proved their skills with an ash shortbow were brought into the forest by a Wild Knight instructor in search of more durable wood. The oak stood proud and tall in the forest, standing firm against the ebb of time. Villagers would gather the sturdiest branches that they could and be brought to a quiet place in the wood to begin carving their new bows. 

The Wild Knight would tell them that a good bow is more than just a weapon, but an extension of oneself. They encouraged each villager to put a piece of themselves into the bow, a decoration on the handle, or a poem carved into the wood. Whatever made the bow personal to them. 

This connection in the crafting process would help the villagers feel comfortable with their new weapon, and many young hunters would use the same bow throughout their lives. It even became a tradition for young hunters to gift a bow to their beloved as a marriage proposal. A tradition that survived into the third age.
KeysToEntries.BlightwoodShortbow:
Every hunter reaches a crossroads in life when a choice becomes clear. They have honed their skills with a bow, surpassing the challenges posed by the Brynmoor prey. They could settle into a comfortable life in the village—hunting the same old deer, earning a regular wage, and basking in a relaxing level of respect from their peers. Or, they could dare to dream bigger, to push their skills to new heights. 

The Wild Knights journeyed into more dangerous territories, facing off against deadly monsters warped by the anima twist. Joining them would mean abandoning the comfortable life of friends and family but earning a chance to face challenges that they would never face at home. 

Most choose comfort, as one might expect. But those who choose to improve would be taught how to craft a more powerful weapon, casting aside the oak bows that carry their names. They would carve a new name and identity into the limbs of their latest weapon and join the knights. Their names would be whispered, and gossip about them would transform into fireside stories—ghost tales about those still alive—the first embers of legends.
KeysToEntries.WoodenArrow:
Druids dislike violence but understand its place. The natural world is full of blood, murder and death. The strong consume the weak. Predator against prey. This they understand, though they seldom like it. When they must resort to violence, they choose the simple approach. Man’s strength comes not from their muscles but from their creativity and ingenuity. Just as a wolf has claws, a hunter has a bow. 

When a bow must be drawn, the druids prefer arrows made of simple wood. Gathered twigs and branches, whittled down to sharp points. They see it as the forest giving them the blessing to go on the hunt. Traditionally, feathers are gathered from around the forest floor, taken when dropped or from a bird’s moulting. This keeps the number of arrows low, forcing a hunter to be accurate and precise, lest they run out of chances. 

Thus, death is swift at the hands of a bow, and balance isn’t damaged. This the druids preached to the villagers.
KeysToEntries.StoneArrow:
The stone arrow's weight and durability make it a more popular choice among hunters. The hardness of the tip makes it better at piercing tough animal hides or less durable forms of armour. In the second age, druids considered it a form of cheating, citing that it required a human's ingenuity to create rather than being a gift of nature. 

They seldom complained when the evening meal was served, celebrating the success of the hunt alongside everyone else. 

Even with somewhere as remote as Ashenfall, some humans turned to banditry, and they were very fond of stone arrows. Cheap and easy to produce, they help to kill and threaten anyone who makes the mistake of travelling through bandit-controlled woodland. 

One such bandit, the self-described "bandit king, Otho", would choose only "interesting" stones to turn into arrows. Glittering pebbles that swished through the air. He was nicknamed the "Magpie King" because of his love for shiny pebbles. A moniker he was very unhappy with.
KeysToEntries.BoneArrow:
For a short time, a necromancer arrived on Ashenfall. She had found her way here by boat, fleeing pirates who wanted revenge for her misdeeds. Her skills were limited; she could not raise undead legions, but she had a knack for controlling simple fangs and bones. 

She fled into the forests – pirates having found Ashenfall and hunting her down –  where she would unleash fang arrows against her foes in the hope of stopping their chase. It didn’t work. Broken, lost and without hope, she prepared herself for death. 

But death never came for her. 

Instead, a Wild Knight, who wore a mask made from the skull of a wolf, rescued her. He pushed back her assailants and brought her to safety. Their fellowship after that was unbreakable, though no bond of romance flowed between them. She made the arrows, he fired the bow, and together, they feasted on their victories and kept the woods safe from unwanted intrusion. 

Even now, there are signs of their friendship. Hidden across the forest, miniature carvings of a wolf skull and a bat can be found in places that meant something to them.
KeysToEntries.BoneArrowBroadhead:
Broadhead arrows are more dangerous than their standard counterparts. The tip is like a razor blade designed to make the target bleed. It was a favourite of the Children of Shadows, especially when turned on their White Knight opponents. 

One such cultist, a woman known only as The Athame, would hunt down priests using the arrows to bloodlet them before going in for the kill. She delighted in watching them run as they bled, only to chase them into a quiet corner and end their life at the edge of a blade. 

She was an aberration in the cult. Most members treated it as little more than a social club, and few actually enjoyed the Zamorakian ideal. But the Athame lived for chaos. 

She was never caught.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArrow:
Bronze arrows offer greater armour piercing than their non-metallic counterparts. Copper and tin were relatively uncommon in Brynmoor, so using the resources on arrows seemed like a waste of money. One faction of bandits delighted in this premise; their leader, a man known as the Auburn Ocelot—for reasons he would never explain—took great amusement in their use. 

The Auburn Ocelot would laugh as his bandits hurled expensive arrows at the villagers, pilfering coins worth far less than the ammunition used to corral them. To him, it wasn’t about the money but the look of confused terror on their faces and the knowledge that no one could stop him. 

He was above it all. Better than everyone. 

It was a rat that killed him. Not even a giant one. Just a tiny creature carrying a virulent plague. Money could not save him, no matter how much he flaunted it. He was cremated, and his ashes buried in the middle of nowhere so that no one might risk catching the infection that cost his life. 

Now, he’s almost completely forgotten.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArrowBroadhead:
The broad-headed bronze arrow was the ammunition of choice for the hunting party of Lord Gawurin, the regent of Ashenfall. He and his hunting party would head out into the woods alongside a small contingent of White Knight bodyguards. The lord would hunt deer, boars and other game there, but he wasn’t the most accurate shot. 

The broadhead of the arrow was designed for bloodletting, meaning he only needed any hit to begin the prey’s downfall. A single strike would start them bleeding, slowing them down and making them easier to hunt. The hunt outcome was all but inevitable. Poor sport, some would whisper, but the lore cared only for the end result. 

It’s perhaps fitting, then, that Lord Gawurin would be hunted with his own arrows on the night of Falling Embers.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArrowBrutal:
Designed to deliver a ruthless punch, the brutal arrow is not to be trifled with. It was used by the warriors of Brynmoor to take down more dangerous foes, such as roving garou or dangerous wolves. 

One cold night in autumn, a creature appeared in the valley and massacred a local farm. The villagers were horrified, running screaming to the guard, who rallied the forces as quickly as they could muster. The beast was large and lumbering, and its red eyes shone out in the night. Its flesh was covered in mangy black fur, and its teeth were sharp as daggers where they hadn’t rotted away. 

The creature tore through the guard, leaving a trail of fallen warriors in its wake. It took the courage and skill of a Wild Knight, Aurin, to stand against it. Armed with brutal arrows of polished bronze, he launched a relentless assault. The creature, despite being struck by a whole quiver of arrows, refused to fall. Instead, it let out a chilling howl and vanished into the shadows, leaving the battlefield in a state of eerie silence.
KeysToEntries.IronArrow:
Iron arrows are sturdy and sharp, capable of piercing most armour. Brynmoor soldiers regularly employed them in the war against the garou. 

Village blacksmiths toiled for weeks at a time, creating countless arrows to be slung into battle. They poured literal blood, sweat and tears into the cooling metal, each arrow containing a shard of their hate for the beastmen. 

The arrows were effective, brutally so. The arrows forged by blacksmiths who had lost loved ones to the conflict seemed to hit harder still. Vengeance manifested in metal.
KeysToEntries.IronArrowBroadhead:
There was once a battle between two archers, the human soldier, Eileena, and a garou archer called Krayth. The two were pitted in battle together across the King’s Pass. They camouflaged themselves and hid on opposite sides of the pass, picking off each other’s soldiers as they tried to pass.

As the battle raged on, the tension between Eileena and Krayth grew. Their stealth skills were unmatched, making it impossible for either to spot the other. Both were disciplined and ruthless, their focus solely on the kill. They were a perfect match, each move calculated, each shot deadly.

For months, their battle continued, neither side gaining a foothold over the other as these two archers stayed hidden. Until a single arrow changed the stalemate. It was an arrow from neither archer, a single broad-headed bolt that struck a garou child. Eileen refused to take another shot; children had never been acceptable targets under her watch.  

The pass fell to garou control shortly after.
KeysToEntries.IronArrowBrutal:
The first of these weapons was not commissioned for war. Nor was it commissioned for hunting. Instead, this most deadly arrow was created for nothing more than vengeance.

Against a pigeon.

Lord Gawurin was the ruler of Brynmoor, as appointed by the priests of Saradomin. He wore expensive garments of the finest materials, adorned with glittering jewels and gold chain. So, he was most affronted when a single pigeon determined that such a garment was the perfect restroom choice.

The pigeon's audacity was unforgivable. Day after day, Lord Gawurin's garments were defiled by this feathered fiend. His anger grew, fueling a desire for revenge that consumed him. He commissioned an arrow, a weapon of brutal design, with a single purpose- to make the pigeon suffer.

With a sinister smile, he summoned the most skilled hunter, a woman who had brought down countless foes, and ordered her to end the pigeon's reign of terror.

She stood at the top of the castle, her bow steady, her aim true. The tension in the air was palpable as she prepared to unleash the arrow of vengeance upon the pigeon. The world seemed to hold its breath in anticipation.

She missed.
KeysToEntries.IronGreatsword:
The iron greatsword is a devastating weapon favoured by the most powerful warriors. The strongest garou warriors wielded them in battle against goblin invaders, turning armies of green-skinned soldiers into nothing but viscera.

When the dragons turned on their allies, these blades would be turned against armoured scales. Several dragons underestimated the power of these weapons in the hands of a skilled wielder. Garou thanes tore young dragons to pieces, wearing their bones as symbols of their success.

It made them an excellent target for the older dragons. Velgar, a mighty general, marked those wearing dragon bone adornments for death. He crushed them first, brutally and swiftly. Their strength shattered beneath his claws or swallowed in an instant.

The practice quickly fell out of favour.
KeysToEntries.IronGreataxe:
Few sights are as intimidating as a great axe hefted toward you. It can only be carried in a pose that exudes raw strength, and a bearer of such a weapon can rarely look meek. 

Bandos was also a fan of the aesthetic. While he favoured the sheer brutality of the kyzaj, he chose to wield a great axe against Velgar. He stormed Brakka’s Folly, great axe in hand, and challenged the dragon to single combat. A god versus a dragon, the outcome felt obvious, yet it did not play out how Bandos expected. 

It should have been easy. Yet Velgar’s stone-touched flesh turned away blows, and his poisonous breath leeched even Bandos’ strength. Bandos cut a great scar down Velgar’s side, yet the wound was not fatal, and the counterattack sent Bandos reeling. 

He never surrendered, but there was no victory for the big high war god. He sounded a tactical retreat, seeking superior tactics – far, far away.
KeysToEntries.BronzeWarhammer:
Doric would be the first person to praise the simple hammer. It is a large solid chunk of metal attached to a long haft, allowing the weapon to benefit from the unmistakable might of physics. Weight and speed are a terrifying combination. 

Dwarves, in particular, favour this weapon. They feel it speaks to their long-held desire to shape metal by pounding into shape by sheer strength alone. They apply the same logic to their enemies, beating them into a shape that the dwarves feel suits their enemies better.  

There were once dwarves on Ashenfall who were masters of this technique. However, when the humans began their infighting, and Guthix turned away from them, they vanished deeper into the continent, leaving behind a shroud of mystery in search of something extraordinary. 

They found it and have never returned.
KeysToEntries.IronWarhammer:
Imcando dwarves preferred the forge to the battlefield but were not afraid to join the frontlines if their aid was needed. They waded into conflict with warhammers held high, ready to smash their enemies out of existence. 

Only one Imcando dwarf made her way to Ashenfall, and she came here for vengeance. Her foes were not the garou or the dragon army, but the green-skinned goblins who had earned her ire. 

She was given a wide berth in Bramblemead, partially because of the enormous hammer hefted across her shoulder but mostly because of the fury that radiated out of her as if her hate were flames and she was perpetually burning. In the rare times the dwarf spoke, she simply mentioned that they were looking for someone the goblins had taken, and she would never rest until she got them back.
KeysToEntries.BronzeGreatsword:
The Wild Knight Kessel was unusual in her choice of weaponry. Where most of the order chose to wield bows, daggers, or swift blades, Kessel favoured a bronze greatsword. On the battlefield, she hefted it across her shoulders, never letting the hilt leave her grip.  

As one of the Amalgamated, Kessel’s skin glowed like burning coals, her battle scars resembling faint reddish embers. She harnessed this heat, channelling it through her sword. Her magic set the blade ablaze, a sight that struck terror into the hearts of her enemies. With great arcs of red flame, she cleaved through enemy forces, incinerating them as she severed their limbs from their bodies. 

On one fateful day, she would use this technique against a force of garou hunters. The battle was long, hard, and brutal, and both sides faced great casualties. But it was when she sliced her blade across the young garou prince that war was inevitable.
KeysToEntries.GraniteMaul:
While metal may be the preferred choice of weapon makers, the granite maul stands as a testament to the raw power of stone. This legendary weapon, revered among hammer wielders, is nothing more than a massive, heavy stone affixed to a stick. Yet, in the hands of a skilled wielder, it becomes a force of unparalleled destruction. 

It was developed by ancient dwarves long ago after one dwarf got fed up with the rock hammer he was using to fight a nest of gargoyles. The beasts were strong and numerous, and their skin was almost impenetrable to conventional weaponry; only rock hammers seemed to hurt them. In frustration, one dwarf took the biggest hunk of granite he could find and turned it into a legend. 

Since that moment, the weapon has been refined, using various special components to make it more wieldy in combat. The original creator having done himself an injury with the recoil.
KeysToEntries.StoneDagger:
One of the first weapons ever created, the stone dagger has a sinister history. At first it allowed early humans, dwarves, elves and other intelligent species to thrive. They could take down prey and feast on the spoils, using the dagger to pare down the kill, making it easier to eat. It created the gap between animals and people, the first step toward civilization. 

It’s also the first tool to lead to murder. 

A young man whose name has long been forgotten once created a dagger of glittering black stone. Pleased with his work, he proudly displayed it, earning admiration and envy from those around him. 
So proud was he of the dagger that he would whisper to it, acting as though it would whisper back. On the day he slit the chieftain’s throat, he claimed that the dagger had told him to do it. 

Fortunately most stone daggers are pleasingly silent.
KeysToEntries.BoneDagger:
Before he learned to use the bow, Ulv survived with simple daggers of bone. He lived outside the village, cast out for his silence and strange ways. Ulv did not follow the polite nonsense of human society, nor did he seem to care for their troubles. The boy was happy just to survive and whittle small carvings of animals from fallen branches. 

He hunted silently, using the daggers to slay small prey such as rabbits and chickens. He ate what he needed and sold the excess at the markets for enough coin to purchase simple clothes and tools. 

When he was thirteen, he was beaten by bandits and left for dead. But a Wild Knight found him and brought him to safety. Recovering in the knight’s home, Ulv would be taught how to use a bow, a skill he soon mastered. 

When a wolf attacked Bramblemead, this outcast slew it. Villagers thanked him for his bravery and asked him what he wanted as a reward. He refuted their offers and asked only for the wolf’s skull in return. He fashioned it into a mask that he chose as a new face, one he wore away from the village that had spurned him.
KeysToEntries.BronzeDagger:
Bronze is a common enough alloy, a combination of copper and tin. Durable, defiant, and deadly in the right hands. For Mia Larsdottir, it was a bronze dagger that pierced her heart, cutting her life short at the age of twenty-one. 

As the first of the Amalgamated, she had never expected to reach any of the milestones her peers traditionally followed. She did not expect to be married, and she never let herself dream of being a mother. She thought, perhaps, that she might be allowed to carve a quiet little place for herself, somewhere with room for cats. 

But a bronze dagger stole that dream from her. 

Her killer was never brought to justice, though there were rumours about who they were. Her father, being the chief suspect, ashamed as he was to bring the first of the unnatural Amalgamated into the world. He denied it, of course, but he never held a funeral for her. Instead, her body was left in the forest, where nature might put her at peace.
KeysToEntries.IronDagger:
Iron daggers are dangerous weapons in the hands of someone skilled. Light, quick, and sharp as can be, they cleave flesh from bone before the victim knows they’ve been assaulted. This is why they became a favourite weapon of the Children of Shadows. 

Though the cult rarely harmed anyone -- it was mostly a social club -- there were those among their number who took their calling seriously. 

They smith daggers themselves, and as the blade cooled, they added a single drop of their own blood in honour of their master. Some carved demonic pacts into the blades, though none are known to actually create such a contract.
KeysToEntries.StoneClub:
A big stone attached to a stick; not terribly durable, but surprisingly handy. This simple weapon has saved the lives of many warriors. When caught between a rock and a hard place, why not take advantage of the rock? 

Goblin children play a game called bonk-bonk-bash, a rather unpleasant little pastime that acts as a form of population control. Each of the goblins takes a rock, attaches it to a stick and then takes it in turns to thwack their friends with it. This is a traditional household game that keeps the costs of feeding the family low.
KeysToEntries.BoneClub:
Animal bones are surprisingly durable, especially those found on Ashenfall. The magic of the continent seeps into their marrow, slowly strengthening them and making them tougher. This goes some way to explaining the ferocity of ordinarily docile creatures, but it also helps when crafting tools. 

Cows and similar beasts were celebrated in the age of Guthix. The people of Brynmoor preferred to take an animal’s life only on the hunt or after long years of service. When an animal passed, it was thanked for its sacrifice, and the materials helped strengthen the village. 

A club forged of bone was a simple tool but one that made defending the village much easier. Roving beasts were easily pushed back, and the village was protected by the animal’s last gifts.
KeysToEntries.BronzeMace:
The bronze guard defended the alliance between the followers of Guthix and the followers of Saradomin. The metal symbolized the unity of two different materials and created something stronger. While most of the guards preferred the noble sword, a few preferred the physicality of the bronze mace. 

Each swing delivered powerful momentum straight into their enemy. The force broke bone, obliterated muscle, and often sent their victims reeling, allowing a second strike before the foe could recover. 

There was a mountain of a man known as The Cairn, for his skill at delivering people to one, who favoured the mace over more noble weapons. He crushed anyone who defied the alliance, providing the force of justice with each swing of the mace. 

When he was asked to turn his fury on the priests of Guthix, it was the one time he stayed his hand.
KeysToEntries.IronMace:
Few instruments are as good at shattering bones as the iron mace. It was a favourite weapon of White Knight paladins, who dreamed of shattering the bones of the risen dead. Necromancy was rare on Ashenfall, though not unheard of. Two necromancers had been reported to the order, one a girl with a knack for bones and another who whispered with the dead. Neither summoned skeletal hordes for the knights to shatter. 

But garou and goblin bones broke just as well. In the war against the garou, Sir Lear, a white knight paladin, used the mace to great effect. He crushed warriors, battered druids, and shattered the shields of powerful thanes. He was a symbol of the might of Saradomin’s faithful when faced with overwhelming odds. 

He joined the Wild Knight commander, Vemmeck, in their siege against a northern garou stronghold. With countless goblins at their side, their victory was almost assured. Yet a single druid’s magic held the forces at bay until Sir Lear learned how ineffective his mace was against the scales of a dragon.
KeysToEntries.AbyssalWhip:
The abyssal demon is a foul beast. A single one can massacre a village before the warning bell can even sound. Crafting a weapon from their remains symbolizes triumph against adversity or perhaps just hubris in its purest form. 

Infused with the hate of the demon, each whip crack generates unbearable pain. Victims find themselves overcome with suffering as the agony seeps into the wounds the whips leave behind. 

It is said that the whip even feeds on that misery, growing stronger with each strike, each life taken. The hate imbues the weapon with a desire to inflict more pain, and it grows stronger each time such a wish is granted. Or, so the legends say. 

Only one Wild Knight has ever chosen to wield the whip in vengeance against the demon for a fallen sibling. Her name is dust in the wind of history, but there are still creatures that hide in the shadows, afraid of the crack of her whip.
KeysToEntries.BronzeSword:
Legend has it that when the first sword was forged, Guthix wept. Given that Guthix commissioned warriors and soldiers and, at times, encouraged war, this is perhaps not a truthful statement. Yet, the sword is a weapon with a singular purpose: to end the life of another. Of course, this is true of all weapons, but none so iconic as the blade. 

Children would play in the street, striking one another with wooden blades rather than clubs or daggers. They would pretend to be knights rather than brigands or priests. To wield a blade was a sign of bravery. To draw a sword was the hero’s stance. 

War shatters all childhood dreams. 

Nothing removes the nonsense of chivalry more than seeing the life leaving the eyes of a terrified foe. A sword puts the wielder in that moment, forcing them to face what it is they destroy. To understand that there are no such things as heroes, only warrior against warrior and knowing that one of them will fall.
KeysToEntries.IronSword:
It was iron that nearly saw the end of the garou. Swords forged of the metal were death in handheld form. 

The blacksmiths of Brynmoor churned out countless legions of iron swords, eradicating the village's iron supplies. Each soldier was equipped with such a weapon, and each warrior was ready to cut through the horde. 

The garou are strong, dangerous, and deadly. Their weapons are powerful, and their strikes fierce. Even the magic of their druids puts the wizard's guild to shame, yet their armour fails to turn away iron's wrath. As humans advanced, iron weapons in hand, they razed villages to the ground and wiped out whole families. 

Lougrim wept for the loss of her children, and her cries changed the course of the war. 

Iron is strong, but it melts at great temperatures, and there are no flames as strong as a dragon's breath. As Lougrim bound herself to the service of the Cabal, all that would be left of the villagers would be puddles of molten metal.
KeysToEntries.AshStaff:
It's not entirely clear why a staff helps channel magic. Druids would claim that it was because they were working with nature, aligning the magic spell through the plant's systems. Members of the wizard's guild argued that it wasn't that the wood itself was special but that they worked as a focus, helping to channel the mind of the wizard rather than the wood being special.

The inclusion of rune essence in a staff's design is a testament to the meticulous craftsmanship and artistry involved. This essence, often crushed into a fine powder and dispersed throughout the wood's careful grooves, creates a magical circulatory system within the staff. In this way, the staff becomes a living entity, with the wizard as its beating heart, channelling power through its veins to unleash upon its foes. 

A staff is rarely helpful for less violent magic. They are brute force weapons, where magic is forced into elemental blasts, obliterating the target. More subtle spells rely on the caster's will and are powered directly by their magical skill.
KeysToEntries.BlightwoodStaff:
The stranger the wood, the more powerful the magic. It’s unsure why this is, but it’s been true since magic was first discovered. When used as a tool for destructive magic, Garou druids learned the power of blightwood. 

Ashien, a legendary druid among the garou, was among the first to wield such a weapon. As the story goes, they were wandering the swamps when the goddess Lougrim came to her in a vision and gave her a quest. Ashien was to journey deep into the swamps and do battle with a demon that dwelled within. 

They found the demon; it emerged out of the fog with a chilling screech and destroyed their staff with a single swing of its blade-like claw. In panic, Ashien blindly reached for the nearest weapon, the root of a swamp tree that had fallen in the mud. Through it, they channeled their will to survive, and it burst free of the branch as a flash of lightning. 

The creature burned beneath Ashien’s gaze, vanishing back into the mists as if it never were. 

Since then, garou druids would favour blightwood when crafting a staff.
KeysToEntries.WoodenShield:
The children of Bramblemead loved nothing more than to craft themselves wooden shields and swords and then challenge one another to single combat. They would play the roles of brave heroes against dastardly villains, whacking one another with their wooden toys. It gave the local healers constant work. 

Yey, a wooden shield is no toy and has saved the lives of countless warriors. As a beast’s claws slice against you, having a hunk of wood between you and the creature is much appreciated. Wood splinters, but flesh bleeds, and blood is less easily replaced than timber.
KeysToEntries.LeatherShield:
A leather shield consists of a thick piece of leather stretched across a wooden frame. Wooden shields are bulky and heavy, making them more unwieldy. In contrast, a leather shield’s relative lightness makes it much easier to move into position. 

Leather shields were popular with the villagers of Bramblemead since the materials were comparatively easy to gather. Young warriors often wielded them as they accompanied druids into the woods, where the druids gathered the herbs for their medicines and rituals. 

The Children of Shadows also enjoyed leather shields, which they decorated with Zamorakian symbols, hoping to intimidate anyone who saw them.
KeysToEntries.BronzeShield:
While heavier than other materials, there’s little argument that a bronze shield is a superior defence to wood and leather materials. The metal turns aside almost any blow, protecting the wielder behind its mass. 

Even the Wild Knights, who usually shunned metal weapons, would don bronze shields into battle. Their philosophy was that a life is worth more than a principle. 

Before the peace treaty with their goddess, Lougrim, the garou would often get into conflict with travelling villagers. They saw the humans as prey, willfully intruding on their land and therefore consenting to be hunted. The humans, of course, saw things differently. 

Garou weapons were powerful and delivered by creatures of unnatural strength. Yet bronze would defend against these attacks, and it gave the garou pause, for they had yet to battle foe with such intriguing technology. Battles that would previously have been a slaughter became skirmishes as the garou tested their might against these new bronze defences. 

This comparative peace did not last long.
KeysToEntries.IronShield:
When Glass Knife was caught, she had no concerns about safety. She was well armed as a blacksmith, better than most hunters who tracked her down. She wielded a dagger in one hand and an iron shield in another, and she snarled defiance at those who tried to condemn her for her crimes. 

She was, after all, protecting humanity. Purging impurity. Ridding them all of the Amalgamated stain on their bloodlines. In her mind she was doing the whole village a service. A worrying number of villagers agreed with her. 

As the wife of one of the elders, her sentence was small. An act of community service, doing what was already her job. She wasn’t imprisoned. She wasn’t executed. She wasn’t publicly condemned for her crime. She laughed off over a dozen murders with a cruel, smug-filled smile. 

While she carried an iron shield with her, and it weathered the reprisal of many an Amalgamated enraged at the lack of justice, her real shield was the status she held. Wife of an elder, blacksmith of the town, proof that when an Amalgamated was a victim, justice had its limits.
KeysToEntries.AntiDragonShield:
It is uncertain who first crafted the Anti-dragonfire shield. The dwarves claim the honour, as do several human blacksmiths. The goblins tried to take the credit, but their smithing techniques were not nearly refined enough to be believed. The aviansie might have been capable of such a feat, but if they did craft the shield, they never said. 

The aviansie that did visit Ashenfall only stayed in Brynmoor for a short time. They disappeared deeper into the continent, never to be heard from again. 

The Anti-dragonfire shield is a formidable defence, surpassing the strength of iron and capable of withstanding a dragon's lethal breath. Its previous owner, hopeful it could be a weapon against Velgar, was proven wrong. 

Only the most powerful individuals can hope to wield such an object. Yet, even so, it is said that the shield can be improved by combining it with the essence of a dragon, gaining some of the fury of the legendary beast.
KeysToEntries.Torch:
Mankind has always struggled against the darkness. The shadows hide a multitude of dangers, each of them longing to tear us apart. It is no surprise, then, that light has been weaponised. Simple wood and wrappings, introduced to flame and then one carries a small fragment of the day's protection in their hands. 

Although shadows are banished by its radiance, it serves as a poor defence against steel or claw.
KeysToEntries.StoneAxe:
Trees are arrogant creatures, standing tall in the forests and refusing to bow to the wind. For decades they spread their roots claiming dominion over the nearby soil. The claws of beasts are a mere distraction. Raging storms barely rustle their leaves. They refuse to move for anyone, arrogant to the core.

Yet, it is not the wind, nor the heavens that shatter their resolve. Indeed, it is the lowliest of stones, refined to an edge. No matter how old the tree, how sturdy its fame, a single stone, the size of your fist, can bring the mightiest ash low.  

The stone axe is an alliance of such a rock, working together with branches cast aside in the tree’s hubris. Proof that even the lowliest of us can make a difference.
KeysToEntries.StonePickaxe: It is always a sad moment when brother must turn against brother. When we must strike at the heart of our own people. Such is the curse of the stone pickaxe, whose role in life is to strike at its brethren, cutting large rocks into pieces, so that they might better be put to use. The smaller stone, carved so sharply that it has nothing left but the edge, turns the action of a single swing into an force that can shatter the largest boulder. There’s a metaphor there, I’m sure.
KeysToEntries.BonePickaxe:
We are made of sturdier materials than we give ourselves credit for. The human body can withstand a staggering force before our bones give way to the stresses. So too is it for the bones of animals, especially those of Ashenfall. Here, the magic seeps into the marrow and strengthens the bone to be sturdier than before.

When used as tools, refined into weapons, these bones can be made to break stone and release metal ore from its prison.
KeysToEntries.BronzePickaxe:
Copper and tin conspire well together, forging an alliance to destroy their former friends. The bronze pickaxe can shatter the mightiest stones and can mine through more durable ore than bone and stone could ever dream to battle.

The dwarves that Guthix brought with him to Ashenfall, were fond of the bronze pickaxe. While iron and steel are sturdier, the bronze pickaxe was more readily available, and they could risk a little decoration. Many would customise their pickaxes with symbols of their family crests, or a nickname that embodied them well. Often these would make the pickaxe impractical, yet they became a popular heirloom handed down between family members.
KeysToEntries.BronzeAxe:
Where bone and stone fail, metal provides. The bronze axe is strong enough to withstand greater force than its predecessors and can break through the bark of some of the strongest trees. On Ashenfall, nature has been hardened by the ambient magic, the bark of trees makes them resistant to most attacks. Yet, the bronze axe breaks through that barrier, not by magic, nor by cunning, but by sheer durability and resolve.

The lumberjacks of Brynmoor favoured bronze axes over iron. While iron was stronger and felled a tree quicker, the relatively cheapness and availability of copper and tin ore, made it a much more cost effective choice.

The villagers disapproved when the leadership pushed for iron in everything. They felt that bronze was more than enough for the village needs. But the lord wanted progress, and didn’t care about the price
KeysToEntries.Kebbits:
Weakness: None
Resistance: None

Kebbits are rodent-like creatures that are hunted for their pelts. The kebbits of Ashenmoor are more aggressive than most, though they remain at the bottom of the food chain.

They were first brought to the continent by Guthixian settlers, sometime in the first age, and have thrived in their absence. The kebbits of Brynmoor seem uniquely resistant to the effects of the Anima Twist phenomenon.

Legend says that following a kebbit under the full moon will lead you to your one true love. This has been definitively disproven by scholars from the Wizard’s Tower. However, their papers have been rebuked on the grounds that “they clearly have too much time on their hands”.

Coming from members of the Wizard’s Tower, that’s quite the statement.
KeysToEntries.Deer:
Weakness: Ranged
Resistance: None

The graceful deer is a skittish beast, easily frightened by the slightest sounds. On the mainland, deer are a rare sight, sticking to deep forests and areas of fairy enchantment. They were extensively hunted for their delicious meat and sturdy antlers.

On Ashenfall, magic has infused their bodies, making their pelts versatile and highly valuable. Well worth the effort of any hunter.
KeysToEntries.Magpie:
Weakness: None
Resistance: None

One for sorrow, two for joy, yet these little tricksters are rarely found in pairs. The magpie is well known as the thief of the bird family, delighting in stealing objects that glitter. It is said that if you can find a magpie’s nest, it will be full of tiny treasures. The results of testing this theory are often underwhelming.

A member of the corvid family, the magpie pretends to have no affiliation with the more sinister members of the genus.

The magpie’s feathers are much prized by fletchers due to their sleek and elegant design.
KeysToEntries.Sheep:
Weakness: Slash
Resistance: None

Ashenfall’s sheep are wilder than most, running free through the woodlands without a care in the world. Their fleeces are thicker and sturdier than their domesticated counterparts, and there’s barely any danger that one of them might be two penguins in disguise. It happens.
KeysToEntries.Ram:
Weakness: Slash
Resistance: Fire

As defender of the herd, the ram sees itself as a powerful warrior that can slay the strongest of foes. This might explain why there are fewer of them around than simple genetics might suggest.

More aggressive than the average sheep, the ram is prone to picking fights with anything he can. They have been known to headbutt rocks, stomp on the roots of trees and have long, noisy, arguments with the wind.

They taste delicious with mint sauce.
KeysToEntries.Chicken:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: Air

Most chickens are perfectly normal, content to live happy lives consuming seeds, insects and anything else that comes their way. Their eggs provide the means to create some of the most delicious recipes, and their meat sustains many a weary adventurer on those cold Ashenfall nights. 

But not all chickens are so innocent. Once a generation, beneath the blood moon, a chick will hatch that knows nothing but malice. Consumed with a desire to corrupt and destroy, these creatures grow powerful on concentrated hate. Many fireside stories speak of the evil chicken and the lives they have destroyed. To keep this threat at bay, farmers traditionally prevent any egg from hatching if it gives them the heebie-jeebies.
KeysToEntries.Cow:
Weakness: None
Resistance: None

To err is human, to moo bovine. So the adage goes. The cow is a poor, lamentable creature. Slaughtered in droves by fledgling adventurers seeking an easy start to their heroic lives, a cow’s life is brief and brutal. Those that survive, the swarms of merciless warriors, are ready to defend one another from their destroyers. 

They also taste delicious with roast potatoes, gravy and just a smidgeon of horseradish.
KeysToEntries.GiantRat:
Weakness: Stab
Resistance: Crush

Rats gorge themselves on anything that they can find. Sometimes, a rat finds itself feasting on powerful magic, such as the pestilence of swamps or the blood of some dread beast. Those who discover such banquets grow to great size, becoming fearsome giants of rodent kind.   

On Ashenfall, the Anima Twist has become such a feast, warping these once timid creatures into aggressive and dangerous monsters. The more they feast, the larger their appetites, and they can never truly be sated.
KeysToEntries.FungalRat:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: Stab
Immune: Poison

Sometimes a giant rat can feast on too much magic, suffusing themselves with the energies of their environment. For the putrid rat, they can take the pestilence, rot and decay into themselves, merging with the mushrooms and poisonous fungi that they consume.

These pitiful creatures have veins that pulse with poison and upon their deaths the spores they create leave scars of rot in the world.
KeysToEntries.GoblinWarrior:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None

Goblins were once the minions of the war god, Bandos, until his death at the hands of Armadyl, the god of justice. Raised on the brutal world of Yu’Biusk, goblins have known no other existence than one of constant battle and bloodshed. Their religion and society decries education and self-improvement in favour of conflict for conflict’s sake. To run from battle is the greatest sin to goblinkind. 

Goblins reproduce quickly and grow up fast. Many of their wars have been won by sheer attrition, as there are always more goblins to hurl at the enemy.   

The goblins of Ashenfall have turned away from Bandos, putting their faith in the dragon that circles Ghornfell. Velgar is a cruel and uncaring master, yet the goblins live longer lifespans under his watch than they ever did under Bandos.
KeysToEntries.GoblinArcher:
Weakness: Stab
Resistance: None

A goblin archer is swift and merciless. They have no sense of honour to hold them back, no morality to keep their hand. To a goblin, battle for battle’s sake is the purest endeavour, and a clean kill is the most noble act. 

Archers possess a trait rarely found in goblinkind, patience. They wait out their prey, lining up that perfect shot to send their target to the afterlife. Despite this, they are often looked down on by other goblins for their cowardice. The warriors mock them for hiding in the rear lines and attacking from the safety of distance. 

It’s an insult that goblin archers hear often, though rarely from the same goblin twice. This is mainly because the warriors don’t tend to live long enough to repeat it.
KeysToEntries.GarouBerserker:
Weakness: Ranged
Resistance: Air

The garou berserker is a force to be reckoned with. Before each battle, they feast on a freshly killed animal and ingest a concoction that alters their perception. This turns their world into kill or be killed, and nothing can persuade them otherwise. 

Druids control berserkers with subtle magic. They manipulate the air around them so that they don’t give off a scent. To the fury-addled mind of the berserker, this makes them practically invisible, and at the very least, it confirms that they are not a threat. 

Once in their battle state, a berserker will hurl themselves at an opponent, regardless of the enemy’s might. Fighting tooth and nail, as if each breath is in desperation.  In the dragon wars, they were known to charge at the great beasts, becoming little more than snacks in their recklessness.
KeysToEntries.GarouDruid:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: Crush
Immune: Poison

Noble-born garou are larger than their common-born kin, resulting from very careful breeding practices. The noble bloodlines are stronger, faster, and capable of magic, clearly superior to the lower class of their society. 

Those who practice magic are known as druids. They are different to the druids of the Guthixian faith. They do not follow the idea of harmonious collaboration with nature. Instead, the garou see everything through the eyes of the hunter. They use magic to chase down their prey. Cast spells to cripple their quarry. Incantations to strip the life from their target, guaranteeing the kill. 

In the age of Lougrim, the druids were tempered by tradition and ritual. They were encouraged to focus only on the right sort of prey. But under the auspice of the dragon queen, they are more prone to cruelty. 

Survival of the fittest is the chant they now sing.
KeysToEntries.GarouThane:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: Stab

Powerful noble-born garou follow two paths in life. Either they pledge themselves to magic and follow the path of the druid, or they rely on their strength and adopt the role of the thane. 

Thanes are brutal, powerful creatures, capable of snapping spines with a single well-placed kick. They wear armour that would crush a human warrior beneath its weight, and their shields can stop almost any attack. 

When a thane thinks they are strong enough, they challenge the local king to single combat. This is a ritual practice that involves an ancient rite whose meaning has long been forgotten. The thane anoints themselves with special warpaint and offer reverence to a long-dead goddess before they find the king and call them to battle. 

Most die. 

A king has earned their place by being a master of warfare, capable of defending themselves against countless challengers. Yet no thane turns down this chance to do battle because what greater glory is there than killing a warrior king?
KeysToEntries.GarouHunter:
Weakness: Air
Resistance: Crush

The lower-born garou can never rise to the role of king. Many have tried, but all have failed. The highborn are simply stronger and wield magic that the lowborn cannot match. They are not mistreated by their kin. There are no garou slaves or servants, but a lowborn garou can never rule.

Most accept their place and find a way to be useful, a means to enjoy the most important thing to their kind—the hunt. 

Nothing ignites the blood of a garou more than the hunt. It reaches into a primal part of their being and gives them a reason to live. All worries are cast aside. All responsibilities forgotten. There is nothing but the thrill of the chase and the anticipation of the kill. 

It is no surprise, therefore, that lowborn garou often take up the hunter's mantle. Their precision with a bow is practically unmatched, and they delight in nothing more than seeing their quarry draw its last breath.
KeysToEntries.GarouKing:
Those who complete the rite of the moon, and usurp their leaders, become the kings of the garou. The title of King is gender neutral, and kings of all genders are recorded across garou history. This is because the mantle of queen has belonged to only two beings, Lougrim and her devourer.

When Lougrim first visited the world of the garou, a place known as Antumnos, she was treated as prey. Her hunters were no match for a goddess, of course, and she assumed the forms of powerful predators, forcing them into submission.

She fell upon the capital of the garou nation, the city of Magrawn, where she called out a challenge to all the reigning kings of the garou clans. They answered the summons and she defeated each of them together in a single, decisive, battle. From then on, she held the mantle of High Queen of the garou.

A title she held until Kuldra devoured her, earning the dragon queen her moniker - God Eater.
KeysToEntries.VaultGuardianMelee:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison, Bleed, Burning

It was the scientist, Lacrussa, who discovered the properties of rune essence, when properly prepared. It could be refined into a powder and then inscribed across stone and metal, to create complex magical effects. The vault guardians were one of her proudest achievements. Reflections of living beings, a power belonging only to the Elder Gods.

Though slow of wit, they made up for it with an excess of muscle. Their attacks were powerful and deadly, capable of toppling almost any threat. In the early days of their creation, thousands of years ago, there were no living beings that could have withstood their power.

During the dragon rebellion, it was Lacrussa’s greatest regret that she could not send the guardians beyond the magical enchantments of the vaults. The magic that animated them only worked within those walls, and dragons had no need of dragonkin secrets.
KeysToEntries.VaultGuardianRanged:
Weakness: Crush
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison, Bleed, Burning

It was Avisk who raised the question of whether the vault guardians had souls. They weren’t intelligent and were useful for little more than manual labour and defence. Yet, they possessed a spark of creativity that Avisk argued was a sign of something more profound.

Lacrussa dismissed him; of course, they were tools, nothing more. Still, there were moments when even she doubted. When she found her sibling, the seer Viika, laughing with one, she questioned everything, especially since Viika had never laughed with her.

She studied them carefully for years afterwards, trying to figure out what Viika saw in them. Yet, there was never enough there to make her believe. Viika was, after all, prone to behaviour that Lacrussa could not understand. She loved her sibling dearly; breaking them free of the gilded prison they’d been raised in was what had made her an outcast to her people. Yet she knew that, deep down, she would never truly understand them.

The vault guardians were a tool and a mystery that would haunt her until her final moments.
KeysToEntries.Wolf:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None

The wolves of Ashenfall are dangerous creatures. Their anima-infused muscles make them faster. Their razor-sharp teeth would pierce the armour of the Brynmoor villagers. It would be the job of the Wild Knights to hunt them down and prevent them from attacking the human settlements.

They did this by culling the leaders of the packs, as well as to lure larger groups away from human settlements, with fresh deer kills.

One year, an alpha wolf, known as Shatterfang, ignored the lures and followed his nose to Bramblemead. The resulting confrontation was an unmistakable bloodbath, with villagers locking themselves in their homes, praying to Guthix that the wolves wouldn’t break through the door.

The Wild Knights sent in their hunters, but Shatterfang was as clever as he was cruel. He used the urban structures against the hunters, avoiding their arrows and escaping their wrath. He was finally felled when an unlikely ally used dark magic to lock the wolf’s bones in place, while her companion struck from the shadows with blades forged from the wolf’s kin.   

The villagers offered any reward to their saviours. Money, weapons, gems, yet the pair asked only for Shatterfang’s skull in return.
KeysToEntries.DireWolf:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None

Dire wolves are the apex of their species. They have absorbed more of the ambient anima of the region, making them stronger and more powerful than their smaller kin. Their attacks rend the flesh, leaving lasting damage that their foes might never recover from.

They lead their packs with absolute authority, refuting any challenges with only a glance.

The Wild Knights respected the dire wolves, preferring not to kill them if there was any other choice. They believed that if the dire wolves could be deterred from a particular course of action, the other wolves would follow. Invariably this was true, but there were always exceptions.

There are legends of a silver wolf among the garou.  Some of them whisper that it is a goddess reincarnated. That she is rebuilding her power, to challenge their new High Queen again.
KeysToEntries.Terrorbird:
Weakness: Stab
Resistance: Air

The terrorbird lives up to its name with both it’s violent tendencies and the nightmarish cries it makes in the shadows. There are few who can look into the eyes of a terrorbird and not regret every decision in their lives, leading to this moment.

Early garou tried to tame the creatures, but the terrorbird’s wild nature prevented them from accepting the yoke of domestication. Instead, they turned on their would-be masters, leading to an even the garou would forever refer to as “the night of green feathers”.

Best taken out from range, the terrorbird makes itself known by it’s distinctive war-warble, that strikes fear into the heart of any sane creature.
KeysToEntries.Gyrfalcon:
Weakness: None
Resistance: None

The gyrfalcon is an agile predator. They silently dart through the air, seeking out small game to hunt. Giant rats are their favourite prey, though their flesh is stringy, they are comparatively large meals, compared to the kebbits and voles of the region.

Some Wild Knights would forge bonds with the falcons. They’re raise them from eggs and carefully train them to assist with scouting and hunting. This symbiotic relationship would allow both hunter and falcon to thrive in Ashenfall’s dangerous environment.
KeysToEntries.AbyssalDemon:
Weakness: Slash
Resistance: Air

The Abyssal Demon is a nightmare incarnated. Appearing suddenly out of nowhere and capable of tearing a man to shreds in a single swing.

No one knows how the demons arrived on Ashenfall, as they are not native to this plane. They dwell, as their name suggests, in the Abyss, the realm between worlds. They were cast out from their homeworld by the god Zamorak when he led the demon uprising on Infernus, and they have remained hateful ever since. Unable to strike at the god that banished them, they turn their fury on every living thing, existing for nothing more than to perpetuate suffering.

A necromancer and her Wild Knight ally were the first to encounter the beast. She had been investigating one of the dragonkin ruins, a vault buried deep into the soil, and when they broke the seal, the foul beast emerged.  At least, that’s what she claims. None have been able to venture deep enough into the vaults to confirm the truth of her words.

She and her companion vanished from the area soon after, their whereabouts unknown.
KeysToEntries.Velgar:
Weakness: None
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison, Burning

General Velgar was one of Kuldra’s most trusted allies in the dragon rebellion. In the years that led to her betrayal of their dragonkin masters, she would seek Velgar’s advice on how to best handle the situation. His answer was always clear, brutality was the only language that their masters would understand.

He had been Brakka’s beast of burden. A mighty warrior, forced to be little more than a pack mule for the arrogant architect of the dragonkin. When the war against the goblins and the humans brought the dragons into the conflict, Velgar was finally allowed to join the battle. He slaughtered without mercy, obliterating settlements and entire platoons with barely a sweat.

Brakka rewarded his brutality with yet more chains. When the conflict dulled, Velgar was made to haul stone and metal, so that Brakka could rebuild damaged fortresses.

It is perhaps no wonder, therefore, that he accepted Kuldra’s scheme without hesitation. The first to join her war, aside from her own child. She taught him to absorb the power of the anima font, transforming him into a dragon infused with the might of the earth itself.
KeysToEntries.OakStaff:
Saradominist mages preferred the hefty swing of an oak staff over the more pliable ash. They felt that sturdier oak allowed for more powerful spells. The more durable wood was less prone to shattering itself into splinters when they cast their most devastating magic. 

While most wizards practised familiar spells, pushing their research into different practical applications for what was already known, one wizard would push the boundaries of spellcasting. Her name was Cynthia Valtis, and she would go down in local legend.

She would have liked to be remembered for her ability to transform twigs into carrots, thus preventing the famine that threatened Brynmoor. She would have wanted to be remembered for her clever application of wind and water magic to create a nice cool breeze during the summer heat. But, alas, she would be remembered for an ill-advised fire magic experiment that would see her travel across the continent faster than any wizard before. 

Her body has yet to be found.
KeysToEntries.AdventurersBody:
A simple tunic brought with you from the mainland. It barely classes as armour, but it’s better than no armour at all. 

When you joined the expedition, you had nothing but the clothes on your back. All your worldly belongings were lost, and all your gold drained away. Debt collectors would appear out of the shadows, swarming toward you like rats seeking cheese. What choice did you have but to leave? 

The expedition offered you a chance away from all your troubles. An opportunity to escape the cruel hand that fate has dealt you. These clothes once represented everything you ever lost, but now they represent the threadbare start of a new beginning.
KeysToEntries.AdventurersLegs:
You brought simple cloth trousers with you from the mainland. They barely qualify as armour, but they’re better than no armour at all. 

These clothes have seen you across the oceans, keeping you warm in the cold sea breeze. Now starched with sea salt and weathered by labouring away on the ship deck, these clothes are a testament to how far you’ve come. This is the furthest you’ve ever been from home, and perhaps it’s a chance to craft a new one.
KeysToEntries.LinenApprenticeHead:
Prized for its lightweight and nimble nature, linen armour finds favour among apprentice wizards and those who value agility. Composed of layered cloth, it forms a rudimentary defence capable of withstanding minor assaults. 

In the third age, apprentice wizards wore this armour as they practised basic spells. The more senior, blue-robed wizards guided them in their practice and taught them basic enchantments that helped them resist low-level spells and curses. 

The creation of these robes was a sacred duty entrusted to the young acolytes of the faith. It was a testament to their devotion and a means of assisting others in their service to Saradomin.
KeysToEntries.LinenApprenticeBody:
Prized for its lightweight and nimble nature, linen armour finds favour among apprentice wizards and those who value agility. Composed of layered cloth, it forms a rudimentary defence capable of withstanding minor assaults. 

At first, acolytes would spend time at the wizard’s tower to learn about the magic the armour would protect the wearer against. It was common for young priestly acolytes to become enamoured with the mysterious life of the apprentice wizards. They would see them hurling spells with skill and panache, making their lives of quiet contemplation seem awfully dull in response. Many young acolytes would make linen armour for themselves, practising spellcasting in secret.

The priests soon discovered their numbers dwindling, while the wizard’s guild gained more members. So, the decree was given that robes were to be crafted in silent contemplation, far away from those who would later wear them. This would keep fewer acolytes from being lured away, but the defensive prowess of the armour diminished in return.

The high priests considered this a reasonable exchange.
KeysToEntries.LinenApprenticeLegs:
Prized for its lightweight and nimble nature, linen armour finds favour among apprentice wizards and those who value agility. Composed of layered cloth, it forms a rudimentary defence capable of withstanding minor assaults. 

This simple armour would remain popular with apprentice wizards long into the third age, falling out of fashion only when faced with something more dangerous than a goblin.

The beastmen further north of Brynmoor used weapons far more vicious than a goblin might wield. They tore through linen armour as if it were nothing, and the diminished enchantments of the acolytes did nothing to shield the blows.

Apprentice wizards died in droves, and the armour fell out of favour.
KeysToEntries.LeatherArmourHead:
With a distinct aroma, leather armour became very popular with warriors and hunters, as well as a few villagers, but that’s another story. Leather, when properly layered and cured, can turn away both the claws of a beast and the blades of an assailant.

It takes time and skill to craft leather armour. The material needs to be cured, cut, shaped and then stitched together with more durable material than simple thread. It can be stiff to move and surprisingly heavy, something young villagers would discover on their earliest hunts.

Young hunters would be gifted leather armour by their family as a sign of their adulthood. Good quality leather armour showed great pride in one’s progeny. Many villagers would compete with one another to gift more elaborate armour as if doing so was a symbol to all others that their child was special.
KeysToEntries.LeatherArmourBody:
With a distinct aroma, leather armour became very popular with warriors and hunters, as well as a few villagers, but that’s another story. Leather, when properly layered and cured, can turn away both the claws of a beast and the blades of an assailant.

Some craftsmen would create more elaborate armour, trading functionality for impressive style. With the discovery of anima twisted plants that existed in vibrant hues, new dyes were crafted, allowing for colourful and eye-catching armour.
This would prove problematic when attempting to be camouflaged on the hunt. Many a colourful set of armour would be found in tattered shreds in the woods. The owner of the armour lost to the wilds.
KeysToEntries.LeatherArmourLegs:
With a distinct aroma, leather armour became very popular with warriors and hunters, as well as a few villagers, but that’s another story. Leather, when properly layered and cured, can turn away both the claws of a beast and the blades of an assailant.

Brynmoor villagers favoured simpler styles, which were easy to produce and focused on defence. When traversing the wild forests of Ashenfall, they quickly found that more elaborate designs got in the way.
