It was better to keep things simple so that they might be more easily repaired than to choose style over function.

Still, it was common for villagers to add small adornments to their leather armour to make them stand out. Patches made to resemble animals were common practices, though flowers were also popular.

Gentle perfumes were also favoured to help limit the leather’s distinctive smell.
KeysToEntries.WizardRobeHead:
These blue robes are the signature clothing worn by every respectable mage. Apprentices look forward to the day they first wear these, as it shows they have graduated to full wizard status. The wizard's tower, in particular, uses these robes as a uniform, signifying their magical power and fealty to the god of order, Saradomin.

On Ashenfall, it was rare for those wearing these robes to be seen outside the wizard's guild. The structure on the hillside was home to strange practices and even stranger experiments. Powerful spells were studied and unleashed across an already magically problematic countryside.

The White Knights, often summoned to address the perils that the wizard's guild inadvertently unleashed, were a frequent presence. Their duties ranged from quelling peculiar weather phenomena to managing the 'accidental' summoning of demons. To maintain control, a contingent of knights established a permanent residence within the guild, their vigilant eyes ever watchful.
KeysToEntries.WizardRobeBody:
These blue robes are the signature clothing worn by every respectable mage. Apprentices look forward to the day they first wear these, as it shows they have graduated to full wizard status. The wizard's tower, in particular, uses these robes as a uniform, signifying their magical power and fealty to the god of order, Saradomin.

Despite the antagonistic intentions of the White Knights' arrival in the Wizard’s Guild, the two factions got along well. Apprentices would often prank low-ranked knights, using small spells to make them appear foolish. The knights would respond with more practical reprisals, which led to an escalation of shenanigans until the highest-ranking members of both orders got involved. Then things got really out of hand.
KeysToEntries.WizardRobeLegs:
These blue robes are the signature clothing worn by every respectable mage. Apprentices look forward to the day they first wear these, as it shows they have graduated to full wizard status. The wizard's tower, in particular, uses these robes as a uniform, signifying their magical power and fealty to the god of order, Saradomin.

All documents have been expunged of the events that came to be known as the Day of Purple Feathers. No wizard or knight will speak of what occurred. However, villagers soon learned that squawking like a chicken would send both knight and wizard into unexplained panic.

For some reason, the egg deliveries to the Wizard's guild were cancelled around the same time.
KeysToEntries.HardLeatherArmourHead:
Hardened leather is a sturdier defence, able to turn away more powerful attacks. Favoured by hunters and rangers, who like the defence it offers without the reduction in mobility brought on by sturdier armour.

Wild Knight hunters would favour this armour when visiting the forests of Brynmoor. Hard leather was sturdy, and the colours blended more easily with the treeline, allowing them to stalk their prey more stealthily.

For those of Amalgamated heritage, the armour was more easily shaped around any inconvenient mutations, and many a ranger would theme their armour to match. Fire-touched amalgamated were poorly suited to the armour, cooking the leather as they went. But those affected by plant-like mutations would enjoy blending leather and leaf.
KeysToEntries.HardLeatherArmourBody:
Hardened leather is a sturdier defence, able to turn away more powerful attacks. Favoured by hunters and rangers, who like the defence it offers without the reduction in mobility brought on by sturdier armour.

During the schism and the inner conflicts between ‘pure’ humans and the Amalgamated, Wild Knights would sneak into the village and rescue persecuted Amalgamated under the dead of night. Their armour was silent enough to allow them to move through the village quietly, and they had enough mobility to make a quick escape if caught.

Those who captured Amalgamated were rarely well-equipped soldiers, and their knives were often turned away by the hardness of the leather. During those dark times, this simple armour saved many lives.
KeysToEntries.HardLeatherArmourLegs:
Hardened leather is a sturdier defence, able to turn away more powerful attacks. Favoured by hunters and rangers, who like the defence it offers without the reduction in mobility brought on by sturdier armour.

In poor weather, particularly rain and cold, the armour would create a distinctive creaking noise. Barely audible for the most part, but in the silence of night it felt like a deafening roar. Wild Knights made up for the sound of their armour, as they crept through the streets to rescue Amalgamated, by using simple spells to distract people.

They called animal spirits, notably crows, ravens and magpies, which would caw angrily at anyone nearby. This would earn the term “dark tidings”, referring to whenever the Wild Knights were seen near town – in reference to the magpie spirits they would summon.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArmourHead:
Bronze was once a secret held by the ancient dwarves. It is a more robust metal forged from an alliance of two others. 

This union of metals was often used as a symbol of cooperation. Alliances would store the signed documents in bronze scroll cases or sign them with a pen made of tin and another of copper. 

On Ashenfall, it was used to show that the old ways could stand beside the new. For a while, in the third age, druids wore pins of copper, and the Saradominist priests wore tin pins. They would then stand together, side by side, in village council meetings, a promise that neither one stood stronger than the other.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArmourBody:
Bronze was once a secret held by the ancient dwarves. It is a more robust metal forged from an alliance of two others. 

The alliance of two different faiths had its own warrior force to defend its shared ideals. Clad in polished bronze armour, these warriors stood out against the stark green of Brynmoor’s fields. They upheld traditions and punished those who blasphemed against either faith. 

The bronze advisors, as they were known, would offer advice from both a Saraodminist and a Guthixian viewpoint—order and balance side by side. Such was the intention, but the scales were already tipped. Policies began to slip more and more in favour of the Saradominist approach until the Guthixian faith slowly dwindled as the Saradominist view took centre stage.
KeysToEntries.BronzeArmourLegs:
Bronze was once a secret held by the ancient dwarves. It is a more robust metal forged from an alliance of two others. 

As the Saradomin faith became more popular, the bronze-clad inquisition enforced Saradomin’s laws, even when they conflicted with the Guthixian faith. It was a slow change, but they went from clearing shrines of monsters to clearing shrines of the Guthixian heresy. 

When the druids finally rebelled, the bronze-clad enforcers were called against them, not the White Knights. The cruel irony of being arrested by the force created to protect them.
KeysToEntries.IronArmourHead:
Blacksmiths everywhere favour iron as a material to forge with. It is strong, durable, and malleable when hot and yet rigid when cool. It has been used to create weapons and unbreakable containers. Yet, it is most popular when turned into protection for those forced to ride into battle.

During the third age, the blacksmiths who travelled with the Saradominist priests brought the technique of iron to the people of Ashenfall. Previously relying on leather, bone and more easily extracted metals, the introduction of iron brought great change to the village's way of life.

They crafted more robust tools, mightier weapons and armour that practically erased the natural fear of heading into the woods. Only the Wild Knights opposed this new material, fearing its sudden popularity would impact the continent's ecosystem.
KeysToEntries.IronArmourBody:
Blacksmiths everywhere favour iron as a material to forge with. It is strong, durable, and malleable when hot and yet rigid when cool. It has been used to create weapons and unbreakable containers. Yet, it is most popular when turned into protection for those forced to ride into battle.

The desire for iron was so strong in the village of Bramblemead that they exhausted all the iron deposits in Brynmoor. The Wild Knights cautioned them to look for more sustainable methods. Still, the villagers had become too accustomed to the luxury of iron.

Militia groups began to form to search Ghornfell for the precious metal ore. They marched in large groups, crushing plants beneath their metal heels and destroying whole areas of woodland in search of iron ore.

Their noise and bluster drew the ire of roving Garou, who saw the invasion of their forests as a violation of their treaties.
KeysToEntries.IronArmourLegs:
Blacksmiths everywhere favour iron as a material to forge with. It is strong, durable, and malleable when hot and yet rigid when cool. It has been used to create weapons and unbreakable containers. Yet, it is most popular when turned into protection for those forced to ride into battle.

From the outset, the search for iron ignited a fierce conflict between the humans and the Garou. Each incursion escalated the tension, pushing both sides perilously close to the brink of war. However, the situation reached a critical juncture when Bandos dispatched an emissary to the villagers of Brynmoor, offering a solution to their Garou predicament.

The Saradominist priests met with emissaries of Bandos and came to an alliance. The humans would claim the mines of Ghornfell, as long as Bandos could claim the head of the Garou goddess, Lougrim.
A win-win, as far as the priests were concerned.
KeysToEntries.ReinforcedLeatherHead:
Reinforced leather, designed to withstand powerful attacks. Layers of stiffened leather folded together to strengthen one another, turning singular sheets into a powerful defensive force. 

While metal is the preferred material for armour, leather has its charm. Its wearers boast greater agility and find it easier to hide the sounds of their movements than they can in metal gear. But its most important benefit is the relative cheapness in construction.
KeysToEntries.ReinforcedLeatherBody:
Reinforced leather, designed to withstand powerful attacks. Layers of stiffened leather folded together to strengthen one another, turning singular sheets into a powerful defensive force. 

There was a bountiful supply of leather in Brynmoor, much to the chagrin of cows. Young villagers would be crafted sturdy leather armour when they trained with the guard. Few villagers would ever make the cut of town guard; the demands were strict, and the discipline required was more than many could handle. Yet, those who made the cut were afforded the uttermost respect among the people. 
At least those who were not afflicted with the curse of the Amalgamated.
KeysToEntries.ReinforcedLeatherLegs:
Reinforced leather, designed to withstand powerful attacks. Layers of stiffened leather folded together to strengthen one another, turning singular sheets into a powerful defensive force. 

When an Amalgamated rose through the ranks of the guard, a tricky feat given the prejudice against them, it did not instil the populace with a sense of comfort. The villagers saw the marks of the elemental corruption on the guards and would turn and walk the other way. They would refuse to report a crime if they saw leaves instead of hair. 
Amalgamated guards would rarely be granted equipment of greater quality than reinforced leather. No matter what missions they were assigned. It was seen as a waste, considering the uncooperative nature of many villagers, making it hard for an Amalgamated guard to do their duty. 

It is, perhaps, no wonder that few Amalgamated guards chose to remain in the service for long.
KeysToEntries.DarkMageHead:
Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

The first set of dark mage robes were so named because the technique to craft them required cloth that had been dyed by a unique alchemical mixture. As the years passed and Guthix introduced runes, the mixture was replaced with subtle enchantments, though the appearance was kept the same for tradition’s sake. 

Naturally, many practitioners of magic preferred this armour because of its darker aesthetic. Indeed, many rogue “dark wizards” would don this armour and use magic to engage in sinister undertakings, earning this armour’s moniker a more sinister meaning.
KeysToEntries.DarkMageBody:
Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

Brynmoor had a string of so-called “dark wizards.” Most were little more than bandits with a penchant for magic. The guards were enough to deal with most troublemakers, but a small group of them caused real trouble. 
They were members of the “Children of Shadows”, a Zamorakian cult that began to spread through the population. The cult fed on distrust, paranoia and greed, some very human traits. Among this number were three apprentices of the wizard’s guild, who were frustrated that they were not given the promotions they felt they deserved. 

In their quest for power, the ‘Children of Shadows’ stumbled upon ancient vaults, repositories of the dragonkin’s long-forgotten secrets. Here, they discovered the remnants of a forbidden text, a relic of immense power. Driven by their arrogance, they chose to follow its instructions, unaware of the horrors they were about to unleash.
KeysToEntries.DarkMageLegs:
Although the name may be sinister, the robes and the people who wear them are not automatically evil. 

The most infamous dark wizards became a fairytale for the people of Brynmoor, a cautionary tale about greed and the desire for power. They sought power in the darkest of places, and it turned them into something monstrous. 

They appeared in the village in the dead of night as twisted versions of themselves—mindless creatures infused with a shadowy essence. Those who found themselves in their path had their lives ripped out of them until nought but an empty husk remained. 

It took all the visiting Wild Knights to take them down. Arrow after arrow, spell after spell, the task seemed almost insurmountable until the light of the sun made the creatures vulnerable. In their death throws, it was as if some horrible chill fell across the world as if a door somewhere had been opened into somewhere it shouldn’t.
KeysToEntries.StuddedLeatherHead:
Reinforced leather, with metal plates woven throughout. The plates rest underneath leather coverings, with metal studs to hold them in place, giving the armour its name. Favoured by scouts and hunters for the mixture of agility and defence. 

Most Wild Knights favoured studded leather armour over the more durable metal plate. Moving through the forests required as much grace as guts, and stealth is always a hunter’s friend. 

When they were brought into the war against the garou, the leather armour proved less popular at first. While durable and agile, leather has a strong and distinctive smell. The garou’s olfactory senses were much stronger than humans were prepared for, capable of tracking down the leathery smell from a disturbing range. Wind magic helped to keep the scent away from them, but it wasn’t enough to eradicate it.
KeysToEntries.StuddedLeatherBody:
Reinforced leather, with metal plates woven throughout. The plates rest underneath leather coverings, with metal studs to hold them in place, giving the armour its name. Favoured by scouts and hunters for the mixture of agility and defence. 

Dealing with the armour’s distinctive scent soon became a training tool for more dangerous hunters. It taught them to use the wind as a weapon. They would always walk against it, the wind in their faces, meaning that the garou could not smell them coming. When the weather didn’t help them, the Wild mages would cast constant subtle air spells, changing the breeze’s direction.

One wild mage and her friend, a strange boy who wore a wolf’s skull as a mask, got the tactic down to an art. She used strange, sinister magics to enchant bones to maintain air spells, allowing her to precisely control the wind’s direction. She used this to lead the garou into traps, where her ally leapt from the shadows, taking out the unfortunate garou warriors.
KeysToEntries.StuddedLeatherLegs:
Reinforced leather, with metal plates woven throughout. The plates rest underneath leather coverings, with metal studs to hold them in place, giving the armour its name. Favoured by scouts and hunters for the mixture of agility and defence. 

While the design of studded leather made it excellent for hunters and invaluable in battles against the garou, it fell short against those who knew its construction. Betrayal is a constant foe, and once the garou war had come to a close, the Wild Knights found themselves at the mercy of it. 

Though there were more dangerous threats, with the dragons that appeared out of nowhere, there were always some that prioritized power and persecution over common sense. Daggers emerged from the shadows and drove between the plates sewn into the leather. The poison on the tips of these blades ended the life of more than a few Wild Knights. 

The assassins called themselves the “Glass Knives” in memory of the serial killer that took the lives of so many Amalgamated. To them, the strangeness of the knights was a greater threat than the dragons, and they were happy to pay the terrible price that their actions would cost.
KeysToEntries.Leather: Tanned animal hide has a distinctive smell and was surprisingly popular among some of the quieter villagers of Bramblemead. This versatile material can be used to craft a wide range of items, including armour and weapons.
KeysToEntries.Thread: Woven strands that create a more durable thread. Useful in a wide range of crafting projects.
KeysToEntries.Clay: This sticky brown substance is rarely considered the right answer to the classic joke, however it has many more uses outside the realm of low-tier comedy.
KeysToEntries.SwampTar: Once this tar was some living creature, perhaps and ancient dragon or other colossal beast. Time and pressure refined it down into this sticky substance that can be used for a variety of crafting purposes.
KeysToEntries.Harralander: A bitter tasting herb that makes for a poor tea, yet is often used as a morning pick-me-up by people with no tastebuds. It’s useful in crafting certain potions.
KeysToEntries.Marrentil: A sweet smelling herb that is used as an ingredient in many poison remedies.
KeysToEntries.BittercapMushroom: While many mushrooms are poisonous, the Bittercap Mushroom turns against the defences of its kin and is an ingredient in several poison remedies.
KeysToEntries.MonstrousFangs: The deadly canines of a large creature. Much more fun when they’re not still in the snapping maw of the creature chasing you.
KeysToEntries.AbyssalSpine: The spinal column of an abyssal demon. A monstrous creature from between the gaps in reality. It pulses with hatred, and you’re convinced it wriggles occasionally.
KeysToEntries.HardenedLeather: Thicker and more resilient than normal leather. Still smells though.
KeysToEntries.AbyssalAshes: The remnants of an abyssal demon. Fine ashes, infused with malice. Not great for the skin, despite what Doric will tell you.
KeysToEntries.CopperOre: Shiny and metallic, combine with tin to make bronze bars.
KeysToEntries.TinOre: Shiny and metallic, combine with copper to make bronze bars.
KeysToEntries.Onion: Often used in metaphors about layers. Also soups. The soups are more fun.
KeysToEntries.Dwellberries: Pretty blue berries, go well in a cheesecake.
KeysToEntries.Opal: A very shiny stone. Prized by magpies around the world, who are drawn to it’s magnificent luster.
KeysToEntries.Sandstone: A rock with a grainy texture. I really don’t know what you’re expecting here, there’s no deep lore about sandstone.
KeysToEntries.DireWolfHide: A tougher, more resilient hide, obtained after slaying a deadly dire wolf. It is said that if you wear the hide of a dire wolf, during the light of a full moon, then you’ll rapidly reduce the size of your circle of friends.
KeysToEntries.BestialMeat: Thick and tough to chew, this meat makes for a filling meal, but an exercise to bite through.
KeysToEntries.AnimaInfusedBark: The magic of Ashenfall permeates the very trees of the region, imbuing their bark with mystical properties. This bark can be refined into wild anima, a magical crafting component that is popular with spellcasters of all kinds.
KeysToEntries.BronzeBar: An ingot of a copper-tin alloy. Highly prized by blacksmiths.
KeysToEntries.WoolThread:
The fluffy coats from the fluffiest creatures, refined and wound down into a single thread. Useful for crafting various garments and highly prized by grandmothers everywhere.

On the mainland, the most impressive collection of wool belongs to Great-Aunt Potterington, in Kandarin. Her sister, Granny Potterington, is constantly trying to claim this title for herself and has accelerated her sheep breeding program in an attempt to mass produce as much wool as possible.
KeysToEntries.LinenCloth: Bolts of sturdy linen cloth, excellent for crafting magical armour with.
KeysToEntries.WoolenCloth: Bolts of durable woolen cloth, popular with mages and anyone that likes a little thermal insulation in their armour.
KeysToEntries.WildAnima: Magic is made up of motes of anima, refined and collected into specific forms. Rune essence can be made to hold these motes, creating dedicated magical components for spells and enchantments. Wild anima is useful for magical construction, but cannot be converted into true spellcraft, as the motes and unaligned and prone to strange reactions.
KeysToEntries.AshPlanks: Logs are great, but their shape can cause trouble in construction. Planks can be fitted into much more interesting patterns, which can help in building sturdier structures.
KeysToEntries.OakPlanks: Sturdy lengths of oak, refined for use in construction projects. A popular material for any skilled architect.
KeysToEntries.Granite:
This stone is stubbornly resistant to magic. Whereas other stones absorb the nascent anima, granite refuses. It exists on its own terms, not the whims of magic. As such it is highly prized in construction projects, as it resists most magic and what enchantments do affect it, last much longer.

Legend speaks of a weapon forged from this stone. A great hammer, said to be able to crush almost any armour.
KeysToEntries.IronOre: Iron is sturdy, durable and malleable. Blacksmiths prize it highly as a material for crafting superior arms and armour from. It is rumoured that certain fae creatures are repelled by the ore, perhaps due to it’s rigidity and refusal to change to the whims of magical beings.
KeysToEntries.Jade:
The more green the colour of jade, the higher the concentration of iron in its makeup. Popular among many cultures for both its beauty and it’s use as a crafting material.

In the eastern lands of Wushanko, jade is highly prized. Some islands have elaborate rituals about its usage. There are rumours of rare jades with magical properties, each a different colour of the rainbow.
KeysToEntries.LargeAnimalHorn:
The weapons of the animal kingdom can make for useful crafting components. Unfortunately, you generally have to battle against said weapons in order to use them. Many a hunter has underestimated how damaging an animal horn can be.

It is not a mistake they repeat.
KeysToEntries.DragonTooth:
In the Highlands of Ghornfell lay the bones of titan wyrms. These dread creatures were once the pride of the dragonkin forces. Massive beasts, with wingspans that blotted out the sun and claws that could tear down a fortress. They were destroyed in a cataclysmic battle against a powerful mage with a command over lightning, who summoned a terrible storm that still rages to this day.

The wyrms are dead now. At least, we hope they are. All that remain are their bones. The teeth are particularly prized and are a vital component in some dragonkin craftwork.
KeysToEntries.ShockingPlantBulb: It seems almost unfair that plants could become electrified – a secret guarded closely by eels until now. These bulbs still contain a residual trace of that lightning charge, making them much desired in herblore.
KeysToEntries.PaddedCloth: A bolt of cloth that has been reinforced. Makes for sturdier armour than normal cloth.
KeysToEntries.GroundSandstone: Sometimes you just want to prove how much better you are than a lump of rock, by grinding it down into a fine powder. Yeah, take that sandstone, you’re not all that tough as dust, are you?
KeysToEntries.GroundClay: By grinding clay down into powder, you can create a much smoother medium for crafting. The grinding process gets rid of various impurities and allows for more resilient results.
KeysToEntries.GroundGranite: It takes a lot of effort to crush granite into powder. Blood, sweat and tears go into the process of refining this solid stone into something more manageable. In doing so you prove yourself a master over the earth itself, though the earth rarely acknowledges your claim.
KeysToEntries.Blightwood:
There is something about the swamps of Ashenfall that encourage strange trees to grow. The result is blightwood, durable, powerful and somehow capable of channeling more potent magic than other trees.

The wild knights joked that blightwood houses the souls of the victims of the swamp, and that they are imbued with their regrets and hatred.

It’s probably not true. Probably.
KeysToEntries.BloodwoodSap:
Bloodwood trees are a genuinely horrible creation. Their very existence a wound in the decency of the world, because this tree grows only in places steeped in violence and death.

The biggest bloodwood trees tower over battlefields. Sinewy spiderlike branches cast shade over the homes of serial killers, and you should never trust anyone that can grow one in their garden.

Leech-like roots soak up the suffering, sorrow and cruelty that has seeped into the soil, and then converts that misery into a sticky red sap of raw emotion. This sap can be used to amplify feelings, to convey anger, fear or hatred, across a much greater range.

The dragonkin Lacrussa was very fond of this medium, and used it’s power to shackle dragons to her will.
KeysToEntries.PoisonPlantBulb: This bulb is infused with deadly toxins. Prized by herblore enthusiasts and hated by chefs in equal measure.
KeysToEntries.Snapdragon: Children are often disappointed that this plant does not resemble an actual dragon. There are no snapping motions in these colourful leaves, and a depressing lack of firebreathing lizards in the stems.
KeysToEntries.Toadflax: One of the most popular plants with witches everywhere, as much as for the name as its use in herblore. One witch, a particularly odd duck called Henrietta, once tried to combine the herb with the amphibian of its namesake. The resulting monstrosity killed five people, before it could finally be put down.
KeysToEntries.SwampWeed: A weed found in desert regions – haha just kidding, swamps. It’s a weed found in swamps. I don’t know what else to tell you.
KeysToEntries.SilverOre: Silver is an excellent conductor of magic. It’s why it shows up in so many legends and myths about supernatural foes. As a precious metal, it is highly prized in the crafting of jewellry and strongly disliked by lycanthropes everywhere.
KeysToEntries.RedTopaz: This glittering gemstone really catches the light, sparkling like a tiny little star that’s dying. Count Valingard, one of Brynmoor’s nobility in the third age, was very fond of these stones and was said to have an entire chandelier made from them. Sadly, dragon fire appears to be particularly good at melting them, so the chandelier is no longer with us.
KeysToEntries.GoldOre: Few things have led to quite so much wanton destruction as gold. Wars have been fought over it. Brother has slain brother just for a handful of the glimmering nuggets. Yet, without the trappings of commerce, it is but a metal like any other. Pretty though, good in jewellry.
KeysToEntries.Sapphire:
Your eyes are like sapphires, sparkling in the night
Every syllable you utter creates pure delight.
A day in your company is infinitely bright
Will you be my valentine tonight?

A poem by a lovestruck Bramblemead youth. It was not successful.

Aside from their use in terrible teenage poetry, sapphires are a vital component in jewellry crafting. They can be found hidden among the metal ores around Ghornfell, or hidden in the various chests left behind by the old kingdom.
KeysToEntries.SilverBar: Forged from silver ore, this glittering cuboid is highly prized by jewellers.
KeysToEntries.GoldBar: Forged from gold ore, this glistening block of metal has been the cause of many conflicts over the ages. Stockpiles of them have been instrumental in dwarven civil wars and there are still plenty of people today who would gladly slit their sibling’s throat, just for a single bar.
KeysToEntries.SwampThread: Woven from swamp weed, this thread is both stronger than it appears and magically stable. Enchantments woven into garments, crafted with this thread, are more potent than those woven with normal fibres.
KeysToEntries.SodaAsh: Created by burning plants with a high salt content, such as swamp weed and other marine plants, this powder if a vital part of glassmaking. When mixed with ground sandstone and charcoal, it creates the alchemical reactions that allows the creation of molten glass.
KeysToEntries.MoltenGlass: Beautiful, yet hard to handle, the creation of molten glass was a turning point for Gielinorian civilization. It allowed the creation of vials, windows, and those pretty little animals that grandmothers love to collect and put on shelves.
KeysToEntries.VaultGuardianShard:
A shard of rune essence, refined and manipulated by dragonkin alchemy. Tiny lines, like veins, run through the shard in very specific patterns, allowing the precise control of anima flow through the construction. When used correctly, these shards can create magical circuits, allowing the vault guardians to move, sense and attack with impunity.

It was the dragonkin Lacrussa that discovered this technology and it paved the way for powerful servitors to be constructed. Ultimately she would consider the technology a failure, albeit a useful one.
KeysToEntries.VaultGuardianCore:
A heart of alchemically modified rune essence. Layers of complex enchantments, and anima veins run through this core. It allows it to absorb ambient anima from its surroundings, which in term power the various functions of the vault guardian servitors.

This marvel of dragonkin science had revolutionary potential. The ability to create power sources that thrived off of the ambient anima of the region. Ashenfall, with it’s surfeit of magical power, was the perfect place for such a technology and the dragonkin would apply it to all of their arcane machinery.
KeysToEntries.BirdFillet: These fillets go surprisingly well with most side dishes. The texture is pleasant, the flavour is subtle and not too overpowering. They go particularly well with garlic.
KeysToEntries.BakedPotato: The simplicity. The elegance. Few things in life are quite as dependable as a good old baked potato. Crispy exterior, fluffy interior, and it goes well with just about anything.
KeysToEntries.FriedCabbage:
Brassica Prime – the god of cabbages – gifted the world with this delicious vegetable, to lead the faithful on the noble path of nutrition. In his infinite, leafy, wisdom, he taught the early settlers about how one might fry the leaves, so that they would be even more delicious.

Now these fried cabbages offer those who consume them, the strength and stamina needed to brave the hardships of Ashenfall.
KeysToEntries.SearedFarmSteak: Sizzling on the grill, there is little that has united middle-aged villagers than a steak seared to perfection. Brynmoor villagers would gather around grills to discuss hunting, village politics and anything else that came to mind, all as an excuse to silently judge the way the current chef prepared this simple meal.
KeysToEntries.MixedMeatPlatter:
If you thought the mixed grill was a monumental mountain of meat, then the mixed platter makes it seem like a molehill. Thick and juicy steak, mixed with other delicious meat dishes.

Garou would regularly make dishes such as this. Mountains of meat, crafted from their latest hunts. They would flavour the dish with local herbs, and perhaps threaten it with a vegetable once in a while.
KeysToEntries.WildPie: A delicious pastry crust seals the flavours inside. Warm and filling, each bite designed to fill you with comfort. The flavour is earthy, and rich, letting you shrug off almost anything.
KeysToEntries.WeakHealingPotion:
Alchemists have long strived to create potions to rid mankind of all their ills. Normally these potions are regeneratives, that boost the body’s ability to repair itself by drawing in the ambient anima and infusing it into the blood and tissue of the drinker. On the mainland this changes healing from a matter of days, to a matter of hours. On Ashenfall the results are far more pronounced.

The ambient anima of the region is so strong, that wounds heal in moments. Cuts vanish as if they never were, the wounds healing so quickly that even scars cannot form. The weaker potions burn themselves out before they can heal massive injuries, but the results are impressive none the less.
KeysToEntries.WeakEnergyPotion: Some times you just need an early morning pick me up. While many rely on the power of bitter brown beans, herblore masters have mastered the superior elixir. A shot of this fills the body with a sudden burst of adrenaline, banishing tiredness and filling their drinker with pep.
KeysToEntries.WeakAntiburnPotion: Fire is one of the first discoveries made by man, burns came shortly after. Fires warm, they illuminate and they create opportunities for progress, yet their touch scorches and scars, destroying everything they touch. An antiburn potion undoes some of this danger. They add a thin coating to the skin, via the drinker’s natural sweating process, that can resist the rage of the flame just a little longer.
KeysToEntries.WeakAntifreezePotion:
The cold is a terrible force, responsible for countless deaths each year. The chill of winter seeps into a home and snuffs the life out of those who haven’t made adequate provisions. It was the herblore expert Valius Malone who created the first antifreeze potion, that held back the claws of winter’s grasp.

It was only years after his death, that it was discovered he only created the potion because the ingredients were cheaper than the lumber needed to keep his fire burning.
KeysToEntries.WeakAntipoisonPotion:
Poison has always been a problem for hunters. The forests are full of venomous creatures, just waiting for a chance to strike. The difference between an edible mushroom and one filled with deadly toxins, is hard to spot on a moonless night. Thus the antipoison potion was once of the first to be created. A glorious catch-all that fought against all the venoms and toxins of the realm.

They’re also surprisingly good for a hangover.
KeysToEntries.WeakAntishockPotion:
Lightning is a primal fear and for very good reason. While it is rare on the mainland to feel its crackling sting, in the Highlands of Ghornfell lightning is far more vindictive. Ancient magic, formed from grudge after grudge, infects the clouds and fills them with hatred. Lightning loathes people and will strike them if it can.

An antishock potion can help weather this fury. It works by adjusting the flow of energy over the drinker, conducting much of the charge away from the flesh. This imperfect barrier reduces the shock on the system, which can make all the difference between life and death.
KeysToEntries.LesserAntipoisonPotion: While the weaker variant of antipoison is sufficient for everyday usage, there are some situations that require a more potent resistance. The toxic mists in Ghornfell, over the swamps, are more powerful than a weaker resistance will protect against. These lesser potions strengthen the immune system and keep the toxic miasma from the drinker’s blood. Imperfect, still, but a much greater chance of survival.
KeysToEntries.LesserHealingPotion:
A step up from the weaker version, this potion allows the body to absorb more of the ambient anima, which in turn ramps up the healing. The potion allows the drinker to shrug off greater physical trauma than before.

For years herblore masters have worked on versions that might work on emotional trauma, but to these days there has been little success.
KeysToEntries.Saradomin:
Saradomin is known as the god of order. Once human himself, he is perhaps the most popular deity on Gielinor, especially among other humans. He preaches tradition, education and righteousness, and prizes human well being above almost everything else.

During the third age, he was part of the God Wars and waged a holy campaign against the usurper, Zamorak. These two deities remain in eternal conflict, both considering each other their greatest foe. This hatred has spread to their followers and has been the cause of many conflicts across Gielinor’s past.

The church of Saradomin watches over much of the mainland kingdoms, with priests and the faithful advising the leaders of entire nations.

He is depicted as a bearded man wearing a crown, often carrying a staff. His symbol is a four pointed star.
KeysToEntries.Zamorak:
The god of chaos, Zamorak, is often associated with evil. In truth, it is more complicated than this, as Zamorak promotes freedom and self-improvement, rather than relying on tradition. Needless to say, he and Saradomin have been in conflict across the ages.

Zamorak is one of the Mahjarrat, the mysterious creatures from the world of Freneskae. He was recruited to serve under the Empty Lord, Zaros, but he found Zaros’ methods too hands-off for his tastes. Using two of the Elder Artefacts, Zamorak betrayed Zaros and drained him of enough of his power to allow Zamorak to ascend. Zaros, presumed defeated, disappeared into the void where he might rebuild his strength.

News of his usurpation of Zaros is what began the God Wars, the greatest and bloodiest battle in Gielinor’s history. An alliance of Saradomin, Bandos and Armadyl, pushed Zamorak to the brink and caused him to destroy the entire region of Forinthry in a last desperate ploy. This act awoke Guthix from his slumber, and caused him to enact the Edicts that would banish the gods.

Zamorak has tried repeatedly to reclaim Gielinor, using the legions of demons at his beck and call. He has been pushed back by alliances of heroes, yet is always scheming to return and finish what he started.
KeysToEntries.Guthix:
There are few gods with a heart so heavy as that of Guthix. Born to the world of Naragun, which was destroyed by warring gods, he achieved ascension in an act of grief-ridden vengeance and has regretted that decision ever since.

He was the first god to discover Gielinor and the powerful anima flow within it, and he used this power to grow stronger. Yet, it was when he discovered the Stone of Jas, a powerful artefact and tool of the Elder Gods, that Guthix would become the legendary figure that he is.

The god of balance slept through the God Wars, only waking to be reminded of the danger that gods represent. He created a powerful barrier to banish the gods, keeping them from returning to this world.

For a time Gielinor was safe and Guthix slept beneath the ground, drawing power from the anima.

Ancient and full of a deep grief, Guthix had come to believe that a world could only be safe if there were no gods left to rule it, and he laid out the path for his own end. He was slain by the Mahjarrat Sliske, which brought an end to the Fifth Age and ushered in the return of the gods.

For a time the world fell into chaos, until powerful heroes forged an alliance and created Edicts of their own, banishing the gods and leaving Gielinor, once more, in the hands of mortals.
KeysToEntries.Armadyl:
The aviansie deity, Armadyl, is know as the god of justice. Though young, by god standards, and naïve by many others, Armadyl has a devoted following due to his care for his people.

In the god wars, he perhaps most of all, suffered at the hands of Zamorak, for when Zamorak destroyed Forinthry, it took Armadyl’s people with it. Whole settlements were wiped out in a single sweeping motion, thousands of the aviansie reduced to ash. Including Armadyl’s own husband.

Since the edicts, Armadyl has had a change of heart. While he will fight if he has to, he prefers a more peaceful path. He returned to Gielinor to put a stop to Bandos’ warmongering, preventing him from turning the planet into a war-torn wreck. Though Armadyl holds peace as the highest virtue, he was not afraid to kill the god of war.

On Ashenfall, Armadyl and his people did not take part in the dragon conflict. They disappeared deeper into the mainland and have not been heard of for centuries. Though Armadyl has left Gielinor and returned to the world of his people, there are rumours of winged beings living further into the continent.
KeysToEntries.Bandos:
Few deities are responsible for as much death and destruction as the self-styled “big high war god” Bandos. On his own world, he brought destruction and became the last one standing in a planet-wide war. When he ascended, he brought this war to other planets, wiping out countless civilisations for the Grand Sport of war.

He came to Gielinor for the God Wars, delighting in death and destruction between entities as powerful as him. He aligned with Saradomin and Armadyl to battle Zamorak, helping corner the lord of chaos and forcing his hand.

Zamorak’s revenge destroyed a whole region, wiping out Forinthry and creating the Wilderness. A deadly region where monsters and murderers thrive.

Bandos met his match after the death of Guthix, when he returned to Gielinor to start battle once again. He began work on a great weapon that would be strong enough to kill the other gods, but the god of justice, Armadyl,  built his weapon first.

The goblins, ourgs and other minions of Bandos, watched as the aviansie deity took Bandos’ mace and crushed the remains of Bandos’ skull.

Though the god of war is dead, his people still live on, following the dogma of endless conflict.
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheMoon:
This ring glints in the moonlight with an unnatural sparkle belying the magic within. The ring’s power causes the wearer’s anima circulation to strengthen in the darkness, but struggling to resist the light of the day.

Very popular with teenage villagers going about their black-tunic phase.
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheSun: Glittering during the daytime, the magic of this ring makes you feel more alive in the light of the sun. It was popular among farmers who spent their lives toiling in the fields, beneath the glare of the noonday sun.
KeysToEntries.GourmandRing:
It is said that this ring was first created by a traveling chef, a visitor from strange lands who sought culinary adventure. They experimented with a wide range of dishes, consuming foods thought unnatural by some, always looking for the next best meal.

When they found a meal that did not fill them with revulsion, they would don this ring so that they might savor the meal far longer than before.

Now, this ring is popular with anyone that wants to get the most out of a good meal
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheHerd:
This ring was often used as an engagement ring. The magic weaves souls together and makes their anima resonate in harmony. The resulting change allows for the body to regenerate more readily, enhancing their time together and forging bonds of companionship.

Naturally it only requires the bonds of camaraderie, rather than romance, and was very popular among bands of hunters, farmers or anyone else. Longtime comrades would often gift each other with these rings as a sign of their bond of friendship.
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheHermit:
In a world of extroverts it’s for introverts to get by, so when the magic of this ring was discovered it made many lives easier. Plenty of people preferred to work by themselves, without needing to keep an eye out for anyone near them.

Wild Knights, who often hunted alone, were particularly fond of these rings and would often wear them on their long missions in the forests
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheMiner:
Most magic focused on directly manipulating a substance, magic to enhance mining, for example, always focused on the rocks. Yet, the eldest of minors would know secret techniques and talents, that made their work in the mines seem like its own sort of magic.

These rings were modelled after one of them, and it is rumoured that the first of the enchantments included a piece of an old miner’s soul. Now when you wear one of these rings, you feel a sense of the right place to strike and the right way to carry ore, protecting your tools and reducing exhaustion.
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheWoodcutter:
The trees of Ashenfall are infused with magic, which makes them a little more cunning than a normal tree. While lacking sapience, nor sentience, these trees possess on characteristic in abundance – they possess spite.

A tree’s spite resonates back across the axe that cuts it, leaving splinters in the handle and cracks in the blade. Over time this shatters the tool, forcing repairs, which can infuriate lumberjacks when they’re in the middle of a swing.

This ring makes the wearer’s anima feel more in tune with nature, reducing the spite of the tree’s retaliation. While unable to negate it completely, it makes axes last longer and logs feel lighter to carry.
KeysToEntries.RingOfThePhoenix:
Everything dies, this is the truth of existence. Even mountains and forests will eventually fall. Time is the great equaliser that consumes stars and mayflies with equal relish, and every living being always fights against it.

Magicians and alchemists have gone mad trying to defeat the arrow of time, doing whatever is possible to stall death’s embrace. This ring is one of the most successful approaches so far.

The ring stores magical energy within the band and at the moment where death should claim it’s quarry, the ring cracks and the power is released, infusing their host with a burst of magic – undoing damage and restoring health.
KeysToEntries.RingOfTheMule:
Not everyone can be a warrior, yet heroes come in all shapes and sizes. The most beloved villagers in Brynmoor, were not those who through themselves headlong into battle, but those who took a share of everyone’s burdens.

This ring strengthens the muscles, making it easier to carry a heavier load. But weapons and armour feel heavier to the wearer, as though the emotional weight of warfare is manifest instead.
KeysToEntries.Chinchompa:
Weakness: Ranged
Resistance: Fire

The chinchompa is a nature’s bad joke, a weird branch of evolution that makes very little sense. The creature contains, within its body, a number of organs containing various chemicals and, when the creature feels its life is in danger, it combines these chemicals and immediately explodes.

It is assumed that these creatures must have some sort of communal sense of responsibility, martyring themselves for the sake of their herd. Yet chinchompas will happily explode when they’re the only one present, making it a someone pointless moment of self-sacrifice.

Most sane creatures avoid chinchompas where possible, for very obvious reasons.

They are, however, worryingly cute. A fact that has caused trouble for many Brynmoor children. It can be very upsetting when the family pet explodes, taking most of the house with it.
KeysToEntries.JadeVine:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison

The jade vine is an unusual plant, that likes to attack anyone that gets too close. With a maw full of thorns and a stem full of hatred, the jade vine has been the bane of many a hunter.

They aren’t actually native to Ashenfall, at least not originally. They were brought to the continent by the druid Hokistix, who had a terrible taste in houseplants. He was forced to live on the edge of the village, until he disappeared.

It’s not really a mystery as to where he vanished to, but suffice to say his plants were very well fed.
KeysToEntries.Liverwort:
This plant is not fun to encounter, and many gardeners scream in horror when they see their telltale bulbs. They contain a strange gas that reacts violently to the air, which is problematic at the best of times.

Bizarrely they were much beloved by certain dragonkin seers, the mystical oracles without any clan. They would cultivate the bulbs and use the gasses for certain ritual practices that make little sense to anyone else.
KeysToEntries.WildAzureVine:
Weakness: Fire
Resistance: None
Immune: Poison

These plants have become infused with the storms that occasionally rage across Ashenfall. They have absorbed the magic into their cells and are ready to turn them against any that might approach. While mindless, these plants are filled with a desire to turn their electrified fury upon any that come near them, ready to shock anything not adequately prepared. 

The dragonkin would cultivate these plants, the seeds having been brought with them from their city of Orthen. They would use the oil, generated by crushing the bulbs, in strange alchemies, the uses of which have yet to be fully discovered.
KeysToEntries.CliffWoods:
The temple of Saradomin oversees all of Brynmoor, the stained glass windows once glistened in the sunlight as a beacon to the villagers of Bramblemead, below.

In the Third Age, when the rest of the world was at war, followers of Saradomin arrived on the continent and created a small settlement at the peak of the cliff. Here, the expedition could gaze out over the water, looking back toward the homes they had left, and gazing down towards Bramblemead, the home they would create.

Overtime the expedition mixed with the villagers, leaving their tents and their huts, and finding homes in the village. As the camps were brought down, stones were placed as the foundation of the temple, as the word of Saradomin spread throughout the streets.

Now, millennia later, the temple stands proud, but silent. The walls and windows remain steadfast against the onslaught of time. A faint trace of priestly magic keeps the more monstrous elements from creeping inside. It is a sanctuary against the wild frontiers of Ashenfall, and therefore the home base of the current expedition team.

It is said that the windmill, northwest of the temple, is haunted. The timber creeks ominously even though the sails no longer turn. At night, sinister creatures patrol its exterior, waiting for any opportunity to strike.
KeysToEntries.BramblemeadValley:
Bramblemead valley is home to the village of its namesake, a once tranquil location bustling with life. It was founded in the first age by the god Guthix and his followers, and converted to the worship of Saradomin sometime in the third age.

The Keep of Blue Flames, was built in the popular style of the Saradominist faithful. The ordered shape a symbol of the god of order’s will. Powdered metals were cast into the braziers, so that the flames would burn in the hue favoured by Saradomin. Here the lord of the region would watch over the village and surrounding areas, under the careful guidance of the high priest of the church.

To the northwest stands a symbol of mankind’s thirst for knowledge, a great guild dedicated to uncovering the secrets of the runes that had been first discovered on Ashenfall by Guthix himself. Here, powerful wizards learned spells and enchantments, pushing the boundaries of mankind’s knowledge.

Ashenfall is rife with danger and the need for heroes has always been strong. A guild was created, to the east of Brynmoor, where heroes and champions would gather together to seek out the deadliest monsters and slay them where they stood.

Once this valley was the hub of human activity, but now it sits silent, and overrun with monsters. Goblins spread across the region claiming it in the name of their new god. Rats swarm through the ruins with sharp and deadly fangs. Soil that was once fertile farmland, is now soaked to the roots in blood.
KeysToEntries.Swamp:
When war broke out between humans and garou, it was the swamps that formed a buffer between the two factions. The muddy water was difficult to fight in and neither side enjoyed the idea of drowning on the battlefield.

Now, the swap retains the memories of that conflict, swallowing down the remnants of that battle into its murky depths.

Yet, among all the ruin and the decay, the swamp is rife with ingredients for crafting. Herbs grow between the weeds and the caves that run along the swamp’s edges are filled with ore.

Still, one must be ever careful. Goblins roam these regions, hunters keeping an eye out for northern invaders. The garou watch from Ghornfell, ready for any foolish trespasser that might enter their domain.

And in the darkness of night, an ancient horror walks the region, offering only death’s embrace to any it finds.
KeysToEntries.GhornfellPlains:
The anima of Ghornfell is less table than that of Brynmoor, and the anima twist’s effects are more pronounced. Plants take on the hues of dragonfire, making the entire region dance like an inferno when the summer wind blows.

Towering in the distance is a great tower, shattered now yet still imbued with ancient dragonkin magic. A terrible roaring can be heard from this structure and a shadow passes over it when General Velgar returns. The dragon makes his lair in the remains of a dragonkin’s hubris, a monument to an architect long since dead.

Garou make their homes across this region, sending out hunting parties to slaughter anything that crosses their path.

Atop the mesa, something glistens in the moonlight and a mournful sound carries on the wind. A sorrow that resonates in shimmering crystal.
KeysToEntries.GhornfellHighlands:
Bones of ancient wyrms litter this region. Gargantuan dragons that are now mercifully dead. Forged by the mad science of the dragonkin Lacrussa, these titans could well have ended the god wars themselves. Fortunately, none still live in this region.

A great storm rages across the region, the result of desperate sorcery in a long drawn-out war. The thunder here is hateful and spiteful, striking any that walk the land, as if driven by the vengeful fury of its long-dead caster.

Watching over the Highlands stands a great monument to a fallen garou monarch. A king who challenged impossible foes. A lasting memory of the garou people, hewn out of stone.

The garou here are well trained and merciless, seeking nothing but the death of all who dare step foot in these sacred lands.
KeysToEntries.ToxicSwamp:
Once these lands were fertile plains. Great fields of wheat and barely were sewn by the garou that made it their home. It was a resting place for a people that had been hunted for too long, a sanctuary at last.

But General Velgar put a stop to that dream. He was injured in battle with an arrogant god, flesh scarred in conflict, with wounds weeping. His blood, warped by the magic gifted to him by his queen, became a terrible toxin that cursed the region forever.

Now, poison mists float out of the soil. All the water is corrupted by this toxic blood. The air alone is dangerous, tinted green by the vengeful cruelty of Ghornfell’s master.

The garou here have grown resistant to the poison, though their minds are addled by constant exposure. They are more dangerous, more violent and more filled with hatred, with an entire settlement that seethes with rage.

Monstrous creatures roam this region. Ancient nightmares from another work prowl in the shadows, hunting any that might foolishly enter unprepared.
KeysToEntries.Vaults:
Hidden across Ashenfall are the Vaults, the ruined remains of Dragonkin research. Each contains lost knowledge or riches, sealed away so that none but their jealous masters might possess them.

Paranoid and wary of intrusion by the “lesser races”, the Dragonkin of Ashenfall filled each of the vaults with an array of deadly traps and they created servitor guardians to watch over them.

Although time has whittled away at most of the mysteries, the cruel claws of entropy rending secrets to shreds, there still remain pieces of powerful knowledge that bold adventurers might be able to salvage.
KeysToEntries.PoisonDragonfireShield:
Strange magics have transformed the head of a tyrant, into a mystical adornment to this powerful shield. It is still strong, still durable and resists the fury of dragons, but now the shield seethes with Velgar’s wrath.

The magic of the shield is twisted into a bitter poison. One that yearns to strike and rage against any in its path. In battle, it’s possible to turn this poison on one’s enemies, afflicting them with a dragon’s hate.

Sometimes, in the dark, when the night is silent, it’s as if the shield whispers angry curses into the darkness. As if the last vestiges of Velgar’s fury are carried on the wind as hateful poison.
KeysToEntries.Adhesive: Tacky like your great aunt’s wardrobe, this sticky substance has a wide range of uses when it comes to crafting.
KeysToEntries.Charcoal:
What’s better than a dead tree? A dead tree that has been burned to the point of carbonisation. No longer do you see unsightly green leaves, but now the wonderful blackness of charcoal. Highly praised by smiths. Way better than stupid old logs of wood.  

This journal entry was sponsored by the tree haters of Lumbridge, if you’re interested in joining their focus group, please speak to Tim near Bob’s Axes.
KeysToEntries.UnfiredClayVessel: A sticky clay container in need of a kiln.
KeysToEntries.UnfiredClayDecoration: This clay nicknack will look really nice on a shelf, once you’ve cooked it in the kiln. Otherwise it’ll just leave red stains everywhere.
KeysToEntries.UnfiredClayMold: Once cooked, this will serve as a useful template for further crafting projects.
KeysToEntries.ClayVessel: This vessel can be sealed to allow for the storage of potions.
KeysToEntries.ClayDecoration: Much prettier now that it’s not leaving nasty red stains everywhere.
KeysToEntries.ClayMold: This mould can be used in crafting.
KeysToEntries.UnfiredClaySlab: A thick slab of clay, not very useful without being baked in a kiln.
KeysToEntries.ClaySlab: A thick slab of clay. Used in construction projects.
KeysToEntries.IronPickaxe:
Some metals tower over others, laughing at the weakness of their lesser cousins. Iron is sturdy, strong and the foundation of many a civilisation. The metal makes strong bridges to cross dangerous waters. It can be smithed into tools, and weapons, stronger than any before.

An iron pickaxe is one such tool. The blade of the axe is hard and precise, delivering massive force at the point of impact. Few ores and stones can resist this power, which is why dwarves have long since favoured the iron pickaxe.
KeysToEntries.IronAxe:
While it might be cheaper to use bronze, there are some times when sheer power is necessary. For those times, a lumberjack need look no further than the glittering might of an iron axe’s blade. The mightiest of Ghornfell trees falls before its onslaught, and none in that region may stand against its might.

Iron was so powerful, that it created a problem in Brynmoor. The population craved the metal so desperately that they mined the region almost dry. This let to infighting, small civil skirmishes and the white knights of the Blue Flame Keep, needed to be brought in to keep the peace.

It is said that where iron goes, blood soon follows.
KeysToEntries.ChefsHat: A hat worn by masters of the culinary arts. There are no special enchantments, no mysterious contraptions, indeed nothing that should make this hat stand out. Yet, it remains one of the most well regarded and respected pieces of clothing across the whole of Gielinor.
KeysToEntries.Windstep:
The air can be wrapped as a cloak around you, making heights much less scary to face. Once, long ago, young druids would use windstep to hurl themselves from atop the highest mountains, descending to the ground below. Higher and higher they would climb, each trying to outdo the other. Until the inevitable finally intervened. 

Since that tragic day, it has been well known that there is a limit to the height at which Windstep might protect someone. They call it the splat-limit, because druids are very bad with names.
KeysToEntries.InternalAlchemy:
The druid Gastritix often struggled with Bramblemead dishes. Something about the rustic cooking troubled her stomach and she would find herself in constant distress. Even bread, simple as it was, would leave her doubled over in agony and there were many times where she regretted that day's meal. So it was that she created a spell, which would speed up her digestion to near instant speed, granting her the energy needed to survive, but somehow preventing her normal stomach issues. 

Thereafter, with this spell in her repertoire, she became a noted local chef, finally able to enjoy a good meal.
KeysToEntries.DetectOre:
It was a dwarf who asked Guthix for this spell. Ashenfall promised ores and metals that the dwarven smiths could only dream of, but they were often hidden away in the bowels of the earth. So it was that Guthix created this spell and taught it to the dwarven blacksmiths that had joined him on Ashenfall. 

The result was the creation of wonderous new weapons, armour and tools that pushed Bramblemead into a new era of prosperity.
KeysToEntries.MagicalMending:
Skilled craftspeople are worth their weight in gold. The ability to repair broken equipment, and weaponry, was a core part of any craftsperson's trade. But, in the wilds of Ashenfall, such trades lacked the excitement and adventure of more whimsical professions. Hunters, warriors and druids, were the roles that everyone wanted, and so it was that fewer and fewer apprentices took up the hammer. As such, smiths would focus on creating new equipment, and tools, leaving repairs on the backburner for sometimes weeks at a time. 

Frustrated, the druids created a solution to this problem. Using a variation on the ritual that preserved wood, timber, and stone, the druids could turn back the clock on a piece of equipment, returning it to a healthier state. 

Over time, more people began to favour less deadly professions, and the spell would be used in only the most dire situations.
KeysToEntries.SummonShelter:
Transforming dirt, stone and nearby twigs and leaves into canvas, this spell has saved many a Wild Knight from a cold night in the rain. A favourite among those engaged in long hunts in the Highlands, it has helped fight in the constant battle against fatigue. 

One Wild Knight, a loner called Ansel, would use this spell to grant him shelter as he wandered round the lands. He would journey to the highest peaks or the deepest crevices, using this spell to grant him a pinch of comfort in the least comfortable of regions. Sadly, it would offer no protection against poison, so when he rested in the Blightblood Swamp, he never awoke again.
KeysToEntries.FireSpirit:
The aviansie believe in five types of spirit, those of the water, earth, light, storm and fire. The spirits of fire governed inspiration and creativity and were known among the aviansie people as the Gal'Son – the passionate. While perhaps not replicating the dancing spirits of legend, the fire spirit that this spell calls is no less passionate about being helpful. It loves little more than lighting your way and shielding you, as best it can, from the cold. 

In truth, this spell does not create a fire spirit, rather the runes attract nearby motes of elemental fire, bringing them together into a single entity. Each spirit is more like a colony of tiny lights, ready to banish the shadows in the darkest places.
KeysToEntries.AxtralProjection:
There is nothing trees fear more than this spell. Crafted by their ancient enemy, the lumberjack, this spell has wiped out whole legions of arboreal sentinels. Guthix taught this spell to the people of Brynmoor, hoping that it would help them expand their settlement. It worked perhaps too well, and soon entire forests would find themselves reduced to stumps. Guthix was forced to implement restrictions to the spell, preventing any but the most senior druids from using it. 

Since his death this restriction has been lifted, and the trees tremble in the terror that is to come.
KeysToEntries.Rocksplosion:
Kethren was a wizard who could rarely sit still. He found his thoughts flittering around the place like bees on too much sugar. As an apprentice, he was tasked with shadowing the Brynmoor miners, to see if there was anyway that magic could assist them in their tasks. He found it interminably dull. 

The slow clang of metal against metal. The painfully tedious focus on one piece of ore at a time. It was too much for him, and his solution was simple – what if the stone went boom? Imbued with chaotic instability, his spell would turn an ore cluster into an explosion waiting to happen. The result was that ore was gathered far more quickly, and Kethren was banned from a second cup of coffee a day.
KeysToEntries.BonesToPeaches:
The essence of magic is change, this the druids believed. Elemental magics were assumed to balance one element with another. Mending spells transformed the present into past. When one druid found himself trapped in a wolf's den, he was forced to rely on this concept.

The wolf had him cornered, trapped behind a crack in the wall, too small for the wolf to attack him, but leaving him no room for escape. Around the druid, were the bones of the wolf's victims, which had been hurled through the crack like some burial ritual. Starving and thirsty, the druid worked out a spell, using the runes in his pocket to turn the bones into the sweet, sticky fruit that would keep him alive. Fed, on this strange discovery, the druid outlasted the wolf's hunger for his flesh and when the wolf went to seek his own food, the druid took his chance to escape. 

Eating the transmuted bones of others is considered a little odd in civilised society, yet when one is in the wilderness, one takes what they can get.
KeysToEntries.FerociusFurnace:
Some wizards study the stars. Some wizards investigate the ways in which an enchantment can be used to make beautiful art. Jillian Varden was only interested in fire. She would study it relentlessly, she created spells to warm the hearth and light candles from a distance.  But in her heart she wanted only one thing, to create a flame hotter than anyone else. 

The furnace was her answer and she spent weeks studying how one might push the flames harder and higher, so that metal might melt faster beneath their rage. She created a spell that would revolutionize smithing, yet she dreamed still of hotter flames. 

Soot was all that remained of her lab. Her body was never recovered.
KeysToEntries.Choppertunity:
Edgar was a lazy lumberjack, who was good friends with some of the wizards at the guild. He would skive off work, to visit the taverns with his blue-robed buddies, where they would laugh and discuss the fundamentals of magic, whilst their world blurred out of focus. Yet, Edgar always listened to his friends and he picked up on concepts that his more academically minded companions would miss. Magic, whilst it could be wielded like a scalpel, was also perfectly capable of being used like a club. 

With some runes in his pocket and laziness as his shield, Edgar created a spell that would make his job go much faster – shattering tree trunks into useful logs, in a matter of moments. 

He was later fired, when he tried the spell when drunk – demolishing the beams of his master's cabin.
KeysToEntries.EyeOfOculus:
Elias Oculus was an avid birdwatcher. He delighted in nothing more than seeing sparrows, falcons and corvids nest. In the spring, he would wake just before the sunrise, so that he could watch the flocks of birds emerge from their nests and take to the skies in a cacophony of song. How he longed to join them, to fly beside them and witness their world in the skies. But, alas, as a human, he was chained to the floor by the unbreakable bonds of gravity.

When he started studying to be a wizard, he realised a way to live his dream. A spell that would move his vision outside the confines of his mortal flesh, and allow him to soar among the heavens. In addition to creating a spell found invaluable by architects, Elias also published the most comprehensive studies of bird flight patterns.

When he passed on to the next life, a statue was created in his honour.  It isn't found on any ruin, nor on grand display, but resting in the branches of a great tree, so that his beloved birds could remember him fondly.
KeysToEntries.Treequipment:
The Wild Knights often spent weeks at a time alone in the wilderness, surviving on nothing but what they can forage and hunt. But tools and weapons, are subject to the curse of entropy and more than a few times a Wild Knight would find themselves alone and miles from civilisation, with a broken axe-shaft in hand. Death would often follow swiftly after. 

The druids among them, created this spell to save their brethren. It would transform a tree into supplies enough to let the knight return home, where they could repair their equipment properly. 

Although not as flashy as a fire-blast, this spell has saved more lives than most and it's creator is still toasted by the Wild Knights on their nights of celebration.
KeysToEntries.EnchantCampfire:
The first campfires changed the path of human destiny. Shivering cave dwellers lasted longer against the winter, and in time they learned to use this flame to dominate the land. Ever since, campfires have warmed a primal part of our spirit, and many yearn for the simple pleasure of sitting around one with their companions, on a summer night. So it comes as no surprise that the first druids found a way to make such fires even more appealing. 

The runes drew out some essential nature of the campfire, letting it burn hotter, brighter and better than before. Food cooks faster, cold quickly burns away and even the campfire ghost stories feel better against a backdrop of this flame.
KeysToEntries.PersonalChest:
A good bag is surprisingly difficult to make in Ashenfall. First, leather must be obtained by hunting down deer and the deer of Brynmoor are skittish creatures. They flee at the first sign of danger and chasing them can be more trouble than it's worth. Wood, on the other hand, stays pleasantly still and so it was that the druids invented this very useful little spell. 

Now, they no longer need to traipse through the forest in search of deer, when they can simply summon a chest to wherever they are. Much faster. Much easier and you don't have to lug around all those heavy resources all the time. 

This spell made druids very popular among locals, less so among hunters.
KeysToEntries.GoblinWarBanner:
To most goblins, war is like breathing. Every action a precursor to conflict. Every morning they wake with a focus on picking a fight. They rarely kill one another, it would be counterproductive, but they enjoy finding someone different enough that they can justify a spat.

This is what Bandos engineered them for. When the god arrived on Yu’Biusk, he urged the population into constant conflict. Nations became states, that were ground down into tribes. An entire civilisations reduced to platoons and squadrons.

This cape bears the symbol of Bandos, a weapon known as the kyzaj – a brutal blade designed to eviscerate an opponent.

Once this was a banner, praising the war god, but now it lies forgotten and the other banners defiled to worship a new Big High War God. Velgar, who defeated a god in single combat.
KeysToEntries.GarouCloak:
Highborn garou are better than their lowborn kin, or so it is claimed. It is true that the highborn are stronger and capable of wielding magic denied to the lowborn, yet what makes them great is their will to rule.

Garou claim power not through birthright, but by proving their strength over all rivals. A garou leader must be ready to face all challenges, because to refuse even one is a sign of weakness.

When a challenge is made the two garou battle to the death. Mercy is a concept alien to them, once fangs are bared then blood must be spilled.

Thanes are garou wanting to be kings. Each day they push themselves further, to be ready for the challenge that will see them burn moon-bright as king, or fade away as a forgotten challenge. All or nothing, the garou way.

Even after turning from the moon-goddess Lougrim, becoming servants of the High Queen Kuldra, the garou continue their ancient rituals. Might, always proving right.
KeysToEntries.TatteredCape: A cape made from tattered strips of cloth.
KeysToEntries.AdventurersCape:
Capes are stylish and protect against the weather. They can be elaborate symbols of stature, or simple cloth to act as a ward against the cold. This cape is the latter.

Simple, elegant, and functional. Worn by adventurers and explorers across Gielinor’s mainland and beyond.

After the dragon attack, there are few of these capes worn on Ashenfall. The rest sink beneath the ocean waves, are torn to pieces on the cliff rocks, or have long since burned to ash beneath draconic hate.
KeysToEntries.SaradominCape: A cloak embroidered with the symbol of Saradomin, the human god of order. You'll be the envy of priests everywhere.
KeysToEntries.CapeOfVelgar: A cape depicting a powerful dragon.
KeysToEntries.AbyssalDemonCape: This cloak is infused with the dark power of the Abyss. It's probably fine, definitely not cursed at all. Honest.
KeysToEntries.GraniteMaulHead: A chunk of granite imbued with powerful magic. On its own, this hunk of stone isn’t useful, but when combined with other components it becomes part of a much stronger weapon.
KeysToEntries.GraniteMaulHandle: The handle for a powerful weapon. Wrapped in sturdy leather and imbued with ritual magic. A dwarf capable of crafting such a piece is acknowledged to have completed one of the trials required to be a master smith.
KeysToEntries.GraniteMaulWrap: These wrappings are made from the leather of a powerful and dangerous beast. The ambient anima of Ashenfall infuses it with a sturdiness that most leather wrapping can’t equal.
KeysToEntries.AstralRune: Astral runes borrow resonance from the stars, that silent hum when looking up at the night sky. They embody the power of distance and displacement, action from inaction, movement in the dark. Astral runes are used primarily in spells designed to summon an object to the caster’s location, making it a very popular rune with the lazier magicians.
KeysToEntries.EarthRune: Imbued with the harmony of soil and dirt, the earth rune stands firm in a world of mercurial change. Predominantly used to manipulate or control natural elements associated with, soil and rock, it can also be used to reference the permanence of things.
KeysToEntries.WaterRune: Water is a strange thing, required by all living things, yet capable of ending civilisations. Rain is vital to the survival of a kingdom, yet its citizens lament the grey clouds forming overhead. Mages understand that water is a special sort of power, capable of being shaped in a multitude of ways. Water runes embody this fluidity of concept, finding use in many different types of spell.
KeysToEntries.LawRune: The law rune is an odd sort of power, it embodies not some elemental force, but instead it represents the rigidity of reality. The rules in which all of creation operates, are conceptually encapsulated within this small stone. Gravity, movement, location, all these things are bound within, and when the rune is used in magic, these rules may be altered for a time. Teleportation relies heavily on these runes, overwriting the concept of distance for the casting.
KeysToEntries.NatureRune: Contrary to popular belief, the nature rune does not solely govern plants, roots and the natural world. That is only part of its design. Instead, nature is about the connection of things, threading together the way things could be, rewriting them to a state more desirable. Favoured by wizard’s for their ability to adjust the flow of time to an entity, dragging it back to a point when it was in a more preferable state.
KeysToEntries.WeakWarmingPotion: This potion never chills, always tasting warm on the tongue. It's sweet, like hot chocolate round a warming fire and that feeling spreads throughout the drinker's body, shielding them from the chill of night.
KeysToEntries.WeakCoolingPotion: The liquid in this potion is always cold to the touch. It tastes like an iced glass of lemonade on a hot summer's day, or a chilled glass of water when you've been working out in the sun. Drinking this potion makes the body cooler than before, shielding one against fire's fury.
KeysToEntries.MeatPizza:
A delicious pizza, covered in juicy tender pieces of meat, and seasoned with a delicious sauce. You might ask yourself where the cheese comes from, but it’s best not to think too hard about it. Ashenfall has many secrets and those who survive here must do many strange things. No one judges you for this “cheese”, it’s fine.

Just enjoy the delicious flavours and try not to have an existential crisis.
KeysToEntries.Kebab: The Drunken Dwarf delights in the humble kebab, offering it to any that might grab his attention. The delicious combination of spices and subtle flavourings, makes it a marvellous meal. For those afflicted, or self-inflicted, with particular poisonings, the kebab is a time-honoured remedy.
KeysToEntries.MeatStew: A classic meal across multiple realities. The aviansie enjoy a version of this using insects called Tobi’it. Goblins make their meat stews out of anything they can find… results vary.
KeysToEntries.Peach:
Try not to think too hard about the nature of transmutation as you consume this fruit. It's sweet, juicy and the pseudo-necromantic origins only make it a little disturbing. Oh, sure, it tastes nice and it goes surprisingly well in a crumble, but it's a little disturbing when you get that unexpected crunch. 

Best not to think about it. Ignorance is bliss.
KeysToEntries.DivineCabbage:
Few gods offer their bounty as freely as Brassica Prime, lord of nutrition and god of deliciousness. In exchange for this bounty of crisp and delightful vegetables, he asks only for the runes that make the spell possible. No prayers are invoked, nor altar laden with sacrifices, just a few runes and a gesture of fingers, to provide a most healthy meal. 

The cabbages here are perfect. Their leaves are crisp. Just the right amount of moisture is contained within each bite. Nutrition abundant in every morsel. A true gift from the gods.
KeysToEntries.DireWolfLeather:
