He rarely knew quiet. His family pushed and cajoled him to further the Rasmodel name. Then he was beset by nightmares, as two malevolent beings grappled over his mind. When the Dreaming Stone shattered, they all took him. He was their spoils. 

We can only hope that, when death came, there was quietus. He felt it for a moment as the Dreaming Stone fell, and - perhaps - he feels it on the journey over the Noumenon. 

The part of him that was a titan - that part remains. You hold it in your hands. Unleash its wrath.
KeysToEntries.ZamorakSword:
There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

But while the Children of Shadows do not believe that Zamorak has been to Dowdun, they still cling to a hope that he will come. That hope is the only explanation for why the Zamorak Sword exists.

The Zamorak Sword, made of finest dragon-vulcanised black metal, was reserved for Zamorak. It was never used, not once. When the chaos god arrived in the keep, he was to be handed the sword. It was made for him, a token of his glory.

It is yours now. How it is used is entirely down to you. Perhaps you will reserve it for Zamorak too, leaving it in a storage chest until the time of his return. Or perhaps you'll immediately sully it with kebbit blood. The choice is yours.
KeysToEntries.ZamorakStaff:
There are myths and anecdotes about Zamorak appearing to the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach. Few truly believe them. Zamorak is not one for hiding his presence: he's more theatrical than that.

It may be hard to believe, but some have tried to 'summon' Zamorak to Dowdun Reach. Call it the hubris of a demon summoner: a student feels they have mastered the art of summoning demons, so believes the next rung on the ladder is god. Never mind that Zamorak has no ounce of demon within him.

Zamorak can be accused of many things, but he does have a sense of humour. These attempts have been greeted with a gift. He has been known to kick a bloodveld through the portal, or another creature he has to hand. It gives the demon summoner a little spot-test.
KeysToEntries.MithrilArrows: Mithril is an attractive metal, shimmering and chromatic. If you ever get peppered with mithril arrows, you can look down and admire the constellation on your chest.
KeysToEntries.MithrilCrossbowBolts: Demons are hardy creatures. The Black Knights had a habit of calling them 'Rackers', as they'd often require a whole bolt rack to bring down.
KeysToEntries.BlackGreataxe: Black metal was most commonly used for armours, crossbows and swords. On occasion, it was used for a greataxe. The reason is not a pleasant one. They were almost exhaustively the tool of an executioner, when the crime merited a public execution. The black metal was a message: you have betrayed the Children of Shadows, and your death will come from the metal of our own.
KeysToEntries.ShrimpBait: Fishing bait made from a shrimp. Not terribly exciting, in the grand scheme of things, but still very useful to catching more fish.
KeysToEntries.FeatherBait: Some fish are drawn to feathers, something about the colours and the way the water dances around them, it’s almost hypnotic to them.
KeysToEntries.AnchovyBait: To attract fish with a taste for saltier prey, there’s little better than a nice anchovy strung up and offered as a sacrifice to those that dwell beneath the waves.
KeysToEntries.FeatheredLure: Sometimes a feather alone isn’t enough, there must be flavour as well. Delicious, sticky, berry juice is pressed into this lure, so that the taste of it might spread through the water and attract the attention of fish with fruitier palettes.
KeysToEntries.LobsterBait: Some fish can only be attracted with a very expensive meal. Kind of like trying to date someone far out of your league. What better way to woo those with more refined palettes than a delicious lobster dinner?
KeysToEntries.SpookyBait: The ghostly remnants of ectoplasm can be strangely appealing to certain fish. Perhaps it is the siren call of the rivers of the underworld, calling all life to it’s ultimate destination. Perhaps it’s some sort of ancestral grief urging the fish to reconnect with those they have lost. Perhaps it’s simply that they find the thing pretty. Who can really say?
KeysToEntries.KrillBait: The vile ashes imbedded within these strange fishing lures, whisper to the most sinful part of the fish, drawing them to their demise. At least that’s what the Zamorakian mages claimed. In truth, they were simply tastier and more desirable to the fish, who have little concept of morality.
KeysToEntries.AnimatedFeather:
When sparkly feathers coated in delicious juice are not enough, then create one that dances. The animated feather is infused with magic and when it reaches the water it writhes and wriggles in a hypnotic dance that fish feel mystically drawn to.

If you hang one from a window, you may also find other weak-minded creatures dancing to its mysterious movements. Like Terry. Terry is an idiot.
KeysToEntries.Shrimp:
Almost all fishing careers begin with these little critters. Their numbers are practically infinite, so the universe responds by gifting them with the survival instinct of an inebriated moth approaching the most magnificent flame.

Many a new fisherman has found their diet consisting almost exclusively of these crunchy little things.
KeysToEntries.Sardine:
There is a popular game among children, where they hide themselves around the house and then other children join them once their hiding place has been discovered. It was a game clearly invented by very tired parents, who wanted to make sure their kids were well behaved and contained in a well-known location. After all, where else can you fit seventeen children if not for the wardrobe?

Sadly for Gielinor’s fisherfolk, the creature with the namesake of said children’s game, do not possess the same desire to make their captor’s life easier. Fortunately, however, they are numerous enough that one need not worry themselves too much about the difficulty of catching them.
KeysToEntries.Trout:
There is little exciting one can say about trout, they have few advocates and fewer fans. Yet, they do taste delicious with a little lemon and a few herbs, so perhaps we should thank them for not being awful.

One trout was awful. A villain. A monster who rose from the depths to bring terror to us all.

Nah, just kidding – they’re trout… I got nothing.
KeysToEntries.Anchovies: Surprisingly good on pizza, these salty little fishes are a popular delicacy across Gielinor.
KeysToEntries.Herring:
It is said that if you can cut down the tallest tree in the forest, with one of these, then you can gain power and riches beyond your wildest imagination. Still, others claim that only scarlet versions of this fish can cause such a feat, and so the debate has raged for centuries.

Truth be told, these fish are not particularly prized by lumberjacks anywhere.
KeysToEntries.Salmon: The life of a salmon is one of great journey and strife. They swim back over great distances, leaping upstream in search of a cementing their legacy. It is tragic then, perhaps, that such journeys become the happy lunchtimes of bears and other animals.
KeysToEntries.Lobster:
Do not be fooled by the traders on the street who preach of rare black lobsters worth unparalleled wealth. These are lies. For the rare black lobster is, in truth, a source of great darkness and evil.

Forged from black stone found beneath the waters of the Cursed Archipelago, the rare black lobster whispers terrible, terrible secrets to its bearer. It urges them towards darker and darker acts, until nothing of the host is left except an overwhelming urge toward evil.

Seek thee not the rare black lobster, for it will bring nought but despair!
KeysToEntries.UndeadEel: There is something deeply disturbing about a fish that remains to die. More disturbing still are the eels that slither and writhe and wriggle beneath the waters, their hearts no longer beating and their eyes filled with the milky-white film of rot and decay.
KeysToEntries.UndeadBass: Even in death there are some fisherfolk who spend their quiet moments hooking these creatures. They wait, rictus still, in patience and draw these wretched things from beneath the waves only to find their prey as dead and decayed as they are, and cast them back into the waters.
KeysToEntries.GiantKrill:
Krill are tiny, harmless, little crustaceans, whose lives amount to little more than mass consumption by larger beasts. Yet, here on Ashenfall, the krill refuse to be the victim. Consuming anima they grow to unreasonable size, hoping to fight off any wood-be predators.

They are rarely successful.
KeysToEntries.InfernalEel:
In truth this is not a demonic fish, but rather the result of strange magic and infernal energies. The eel is naturally a magically attuned fish, some generate small thunderstorms within their flesh, others blend with shadows becoming almost entirely invisible. These eels, infused as they were with the abundant anima of Ashenfall, became changed by the energies of Downdun Reach’s demonic rites.

Fortunately, the transformation, while a little disconcerting, only really serves to give them a slightly spicy aftertaste.
KeysToEntries.ArmouredCatfish:
Sometimes evolution gets inspired by the wrong things. The catfish of Ashenfall have long been the victim of any creature that chooses to make them lunch. They are caught, cooked and consumed more often than is reasonable, and their suffering seemed too great as a result. So it was that a god took pity on them and gave them a defence.

It is uncertain which god, for none have claimed responsibility, but the divine whimsy could be the result of any of them. All we know is that the fish now have a chance against their assailants, an armoured hide to ward off some of the worst of their misery.

Sadly, they are no match for you.
KeysToEntries.RawShrimp: There are many ways to cook shrimp. Countless recipes that promise wonderful taste sensations. Right now, though, I would suggest you don’t really want to eat them. They’re all wriggly.
KeysToEntries.RawSardine: Sardines travel in large shoals; their lives spend as part of a grand community. There’s something a little tragic seeing one here on its own. Still, with a little heat, perhaps a sprinkle of salt and other seasonings, and you’ve got yourself a nice snack.
KeysToEntries.RawTrout: Bonier than some of the other popular fishes, the trout is nevertheless one of the most regularly consumed freshwater fishes. Its flavour is heavily modified by its diet…which, on Ashenfall, is more than a little worrying if I’m honest.
KeysToEntries.RawAnchovies: You could eat these by the handful if you didn’t mind the crunch. Traditionally cured in brine and turned into a deliciously salty snack. Goes very well on pizzas.
KeysToEntries.RawHerring: A bit too floppy to be useful for lumberjacks.
KeysToEntries.RawSalmon: The Wushanko islands are masters of turning this into a delicious and satisfying meal. Alas, you never did take the time to study their culinary ways and must apply heat to this meal, lest your eject your internalised failures upon Ashenfall’s soil.
KeysToEntries.RawLobster: Although some people insist that to get the best flavour a lobster must be alive while it is boiled, you, fortunately for the lobster, have already put it out of its misery. Now you can look forward to boiling it guilt-free for a much gentler flavour.
KeysToEntries.RawUndeadEel:
Sometimes you have to ask yourself why you cook everything you find. Is this really going to make a succulent meal? Even dead it twitches occasionally, animated by some remnant of necromantic energy. Its flesh is partially decayed and the smell… oh the smell…

I recommend cooking it very, very thoroughly.
KeysToEntries.RawUndeadbass:
In life, these are gamy fish, sleek of form and delicious with a bit of lemon. In unlife… well… they’re a little less appetising and lemons seem surprisingly sparse on Ashenfall.

Still, needs must.
KeysToEntries.RawGiantKrill:
To its krill kin, this creature is a beast of epic proportions. The sort of monster of legend that would tower over mountains and shatter towers with a single swipe of its enormous claws.

Here, though, it’s a bit like a big shrimp. Much less intimidating.
KeysToEntries.RawInfernalEel: Possessed of a strangely smoky flavour, with a surprisingly spicy kick. Best cooked very carefully, preferably in the presence of a priest.
KeysToEntries.RawArmouredCatfish: Looking at this, you’re going to struggle to prepare it properly. Make sure you have a very, very sharp knife.
KeysToEntries.UlvsLongbow:
Ulv was always the silent hunter, in more ways than one. While it was true that no word ever left his lips, his footsteps, breath and, most importantly, his arrows, were all equally devoid of sound. The Wild Knights would jokingly call him the Silent Killer – though over time the crueler members of the order called him the Silent But Deadly, knowing he lacked the capability to argue back. The boy took these insults with quiet grace, never biting back, never reacting. He was used to those little cruelties, having faced them all his life. 

He had carved the bow himself and once walked Ravanna through the process. The two of them were as true a friendship as could ever be hoped for. No lingering romantic tensions. No transaction or debt. No honour chained them together. They were friends simply because they were. Because two lonely people found each other and found a comfort in each others eccentricities. The wolf and the bat, platonic soulmates to the end. 

For one of his birthdays, Ravanna enchanted the bow with a simple spell. She took animal bones, skulls and hides and wove necromantic magic through them, wrapping them around the limbs and grip of the bow, imbuing them with just enough of the underworld to strip them of their sound and a little of their substance. Like ghosts, the arrows would sail silently through the air, dropping targets before they ever knew what hit them. 

It would have been the perfect birthday gift. If only Ulv had lived long enough to receive it. 

The garou can be brutal in their assaults, considering notions like mercy to be the domain of the weak-willed, or the cowardly. Ulv was simply in the wrong place, at the wrong time, and was ambushed by a patrol of young garou, looking for the bloodmark that would grant them adulthood. There were six of them in total, but Ulv reduced them down to four, before the claws entered his throat and silenced him forever. 

When Ravanna found his body, she went mad. An anger unlike any other, a rage more powerful than any storm, waged a war inside her between the light and the dark. The darkness won. Using the souls of slain garou, Ravanna's necromantic curse tore through the Highlands, twisting the clouds into screaming things, filled with a fraction of the rage she felt. As the ghosts of the dead garou crashed about within them, the clouds unleashed lightning and thunder upon the populace below, destroying countless settlements in an instant and countless more over the centuries that followed.
KeysToEntries.AbraxusRing:
Little is known about the great necromancer, Abraxus, a fact he finds deeply frustrating. In his youth he sought secrets forbidden to others and he wandered the Whispering Swamps of Brynmoor in search of secrets that others dared not know. The swamp was ancient and haunted, so the legends went, and there was a magic to it that was different to any other part of Ashenfall. He explored vaults, caves and battled goblins in the mists, but never found the power that his youth demanded. Until one day, he found the smoke that promised him the power he so desperately craved. 

From there his history is clouded, obscured just as the mist obscured the swamps. He founded a small order of necromancers in Fellhollow and took advantage of the chaos when the Withering began. He sought to bind the souls of the dead into his service, gaining power just as Imaru gained hers from the consumption of the fallen. 

But Abraxus was no dragon, and so frustratingly mortal. A single hoplite spear is all it took to end his life, he would have been embarrassed if he felt it had mattered. As a spirit he lost a lot of things we take for granted, sensation, corporeality, and many of the emotions that everyone else enjoys. Yet, in exchange, he was given the most precious gift of all – time. Time to contemplate. Time to scheme. Time to think about what really matters, in the grand scheme of it all, and he has found his answer. 

His ring, imbued with his dark power, offers no value to him any more. So he bequeaths it to you. In time, perhaps, it may help you become a mage strong enough to earn his respect. Well, a small fraction of it, at least.
KeysToEntries.FireMithrilArrows:
The Fogger's Gala was one of the few dates that a Black Knight could look forward to. An annual tradition, it would take place on the mistiest day of the year. As soon as low clouds started rolling in, word spread among the knights that it was Gala day.

It was an informal event on the battlements of the keep. There were quivers that represented each of the night's events, and the knights would name their arrows and place them into the quivers of those they wanted to participate in. Arrows were drawn randomly to determine the order. 

Sergeant Blister was on hand with betting. He brought his blackboard, which he seemed to have on-hand when needed, and a week's wages were placed on friends and adversaries. 

The events all revolved around bows and crossbows. The fog made it almost impossible to see anything, so the Gala was a test of both memory and accuracy. One event required the archer to hit hay bales with fire arrows. The hay bales were invisible, so they only had location names to go by - the hut by the waterfall, Skrexis's bed - which meant firing Hail Mary's in the vague direction of their quarry, hoping that it would ignite in the night.

Other events included Pin the Arrow on the Goblin (as you can probably imagine, a noisy goblin ran about in the fog), Farthest Arrow and Alric's Curtains.
KeysToEntries.PoisonedMithrilArrows:
The Rasmodels were notorious for their use of poison. When they lived to the south, in Temple Woods and Bramblemead, it was often said that you should cover your cup, mouth and ears around a Rasmodel. One of the family was finally caught red-handed (or green-handed, considering the poison used), and the rumour moved to certainty. They were banished to Dowdun. 

The reputation followed them, and mostly became a gift rather than curse. No-one in Dowdun Reach wanted to cross the Rasmodels for fear of waking up in spasms. 

We say that it was 'mostly' a gift, because of an event between a young Lord Rasmodel and his cousin. Lord Rasmodel claimed that he could shoot an apple from the top of his cousin's head, and promptly did so. He handed one of the resulting apple-halves to his cousin who ate it, and promptly keeled over and died.

It turned out that the arrow used to the cleave the apple was poisoned. Lord Rasmodel claimed that he had been using his father's hunting arrows, and one of them must have been tainted. It seemed entirely plausible.

But there was still doubt. And that doubt grew into a reputation: a reputation that outmatched his own family's. Even at the age of eight, the inhabitants of Dowdun Reach gave Lord Rasmodel a wide berth.
KeysToEntries.BarbedMithrilArrows:
Demons dealt with wounds and ailments on their own. There was no such thing as a demon surgeon or sick bay. If wounds were fatal then the demon would die; if wounds were survivable, then survive they did. 

This was an observation of Summoner Flament, who passed it on to Alric, who idly mentioned it to Lord Rasmodel in one of their weekly meetings. As with most information, Lord Rasmodel examined it for opportunities.

If demons sought no help from their wounds - no remedies, no medical assistance - then his knights would carry weapons that took advantage of that fact. Arrows were poisoned, since no demon carried antipoison. And arrows were barbed, as demons would pull them roughly from their hides, rather than remove them carefully. 

It was a small advantage, but Lord Rasmodel took it. As he would often say, "stack opportunities, one on top of the other, and soon you will have an impregnable tower".
KeysToEntries.SalvagePile: Detritus is a wonderful word. 'Detritus'. It even sounds dirty and worthless. But rummage around in it if you will: there may be something worth salvaging.
KeysToEntries.DraconicLeatherScraps: Little scraps of hide, snout, claw and fur. Most people would toss it away in disgust, but you - you stick it in your pockets for a rainy day.
KeysToEntries.BlackCrossbowLimbs: Did you know that someone who fires a crossbow is often considered an 'arbalister' or 'arbalist'? Don't say that these journals don't teach you something.
KeysToEntries.MapleInfusion: The distilled strength of a maple tree, pumped straight to your dietary tract. You might fell a rising fury whenever you stare at a logging axe, however.
KeysToEntries.GlassPane: When you take me out of a window, you leave a grieving wife. When you stick me in a door, you will save somebody’s life. What am I?
KeysToEntries.GlassDecoration:
Let's take a moment to recognise your glass-blowing skills. 

...

Congratulations on your craftmanship. You have the puff of an angel.
KeysToEntries.BlackKnightElite:
Being an Elite Black Knight wasn't just an acknowledgment of fortitude. Lord Rasmodel used them for tasks where conscience was a burden. 

They would be called upon for public executions, where a message needed to be sent. They were always on-call for demon outbreaks. Latterly, they would deal with extreme epidemics of the madness. It was all too common for the voice-infected to move together in a chorus, a large group whose aim was to kill indiscriminately. Elites would quickly put a stop to them.
KeysToEntries.LesserDemonShardbound:
When the Dreaming Stone shattered, the aftershocks weren't felt just by humans. Demons were affected too. If anything, their reactions were more unpredictable. 

For some, it was like a connection snapping - a hard reset. It was as if a controlling force had been removed from them; a marionette falling to the ground. 

For others, it was a fury. So much ash was tossed to the wind. A subset of that group gathered the shards for themselves, and they fought others for their stockpiles. They were the shardbound, and no amount of shards would settle them.
KeysToEntries.StandardAntipoisonPotion: Just because you're protected by antipoison, it doesn't mean that you should start glugging poison or poking yourself with poison-tipped arrows. Your body still needs to get rid of that poison, and it's not pleasant on the exit.
KeysToEntries.StandardFarmingPotion:
A common misconception is that a farming potion is poured onto your crops, rather than into your mouth. While it might sound sensible - it's the crops that are growing, not you - it fundamentally misunderstands what the farming potion does.

The farming potion increases the experience you receive when you farm. It achieves this by making you sensitive to the wants and needs of your plants. You become intensely aware of them. You will know when even a single aphid lands on a leaf; when the last drop has been siphoned from the soil; when a potato wants to hear you sing. You are attuned to your crops, to the point that you might even find kinship with them. If you begin sleeping in your cabbage patch, you should consider lowering your consumption.
KeysToEntries.StandardFishingPotion:
You often hear stories about great anglers who have that one marine animal they've never caught. A whale perhaps, or a darting salmon. Well, I had a friend once who had an enmity with a prawn. It was a big prawn, mind you, and every time he caught it, it would leap out of his net and get away. He accused that pawn of gloating: he believed it waited until he hauled in the net before scarpering.

It got to the point where my friend traded his week's wages for a fishing potion. Not a lesser or weak fishing potion: a third-tier, freshly made fishing potion. He was going to catch that prawn, even if he had to starve for days. 

But the potion didn't make him any better at catching the prawn. Instead, he became attuned to the prawn. He was so aware that he knew what it was thinking. Now, every time the prawn escapes, my friend can hear it cackle and let out a little "doofus!".
KeysToEntries.ShroudOfNoggin:
Poor old Noggin found no peace in death, because even the gods forgot him. When he reached the River Noumenon, there was no guide to usher him through to the Afterlife. No radiant light to beckon him forward into the loving arms of those he'd lost. instead, there was silence and it went on and on. 

Always quiet and forgettable, the universe simply failed to recall him. His name was recorded in no books of the dead. The various creatures that prey on lost souls, never once thought he seemed like a pleasant meal. Instead they did nothing, except to leave him to his wandering, forgotten and alone. 

Then four people went digging where they shouldn't, and that made Noggin mad. Forgettable as he was, he had always made a point of preserving the status quo and to see these four ruffians blindly digging into the soil that had never done anyone a disservice, why that was unforgivable – he couldn't let it stand. So he crept into their camp, and found the treasure they coveted, and secreted himself within the gold and silver, waiting to be found. Found he was, for when they opened the chest they saw no treasure, no jewels, no trinkets of worth – because Noggin had eaten all of them, swallowed the pieces down into the void – they found only a ghostly sackcloth head glaring back at them, with nothing but hate in its eyes. 

This is that piece of sackcloth, and it's not happy to see you.
KeysToEntries.Lurch: Lurch tends to sleep most of the day. It's her metabolism that does the hard work, producing wind that will empty an entire barracks. Considering she will accidentally ignite that flatulence, she's often left to sleep on her own.
KeysToEntries.Tiny: As with 90% of pets called Tiny, the 'Tiny' is ironic. She just thinks she's tiny, jumping onto Rexiton's lap when he hasn't even got the flame-retardant sheet ready.
KeysToEntries.Rufus: Rufus is deaf, but that doesn't stop him from being the alpha of the group. If anything, it puts him in an admirable position, as he's less bothered by Gnasher's zooms, Lurch's wind, and Tiny's barking.
KeysToEntries.LostSoul: A wandering spirit, desperately clawing at any hint of life. Pity them and move on, you cannot help them.
KeysToEntries.GhostTerrorBird:
Nature spirits are part of a natural cycle, they are both the hunter and the hunted and death is as important a part of their essence as living is. In death, they rise again, like plants growing from the nutrients of fallen beasts, only to be hunted and slain again and again. 
On paper it’s a tragic existence, but this cycle of life and death is vital for the wellbeing of any ecosystem.
KeysToEntries.LesserGreenDragon: Lesser green dragons are weaker than the generals, but that doesn’t make them easy to defeat. Their cruelty is almost as great as Velgar’s, but they fortunately lack the ambition and tactical thinking that would elevate their threat to greater heights.
KeysToEntries.SoulstoneSentinel: Once guardians of souls, they have become twisted by Imaru’s fury into something else. They wait behind spectral doors, ready to add more soulstones to their collection, by murdering any living being that intrudes upon their realm.
KeysToEntries.UnusualTrout:
The trout is unusual in a few different ways. It's still breathing even out of water, which you could probably classify as more than just 'unusual'. But we like to stay away from hyperbole, so we'll stick with 'unusual' for now.

It also seems to be eyeballing you. Little sideways glances that could be interpreted as judgements. And when you do fail at something - burn a fish, perhaps - it tuts, loudly, like a librarian.
